questions.q: A customer-support orchestrator routes a ticket to a billing subagent and a logistics subagent in parallel. The billing subagent finishes and returns a confident answer, but it depended on the logistics subagent's shipment status, which had actually failed and returned a default placeholder of 'shipped.' The orchestrator merged both and told the customer their refund was processed based on a delivery that never happened. What design flaw should be fixed first?

options.A: Make the billing and logistics subagents run sequentially so that billing always executes after logistics has finished returning its shipment status
options.B: Eliminate the silent default entirely: when the logistics lookup fails it must return an explicit failure or 'unknown' status rather than a placeholder of 'shipped', and the billing subagent must be designed to withhold the refund and not proceed whenever the shipment status it depends on is unconfirmed or unavailable
options.C: Increase the orchestrator's sampling temperature so it explores more possible interpretations of the subagent outputs before it merges them into a reply
options.D: Add billing subagents that independently recompute and cross-check the refund amount before merging

questions.explanation: The root cause is a silent default masquerading as real data, which corrupted the merge. B is correct: error context must be truthful — an explicit failure/unknown status lets the orchestrator and billing logic withhold the refund until shipment is confirmed, instead of acting on a fabricated value. D adds redundancy to the wrong layer; the refund amount was never the problem, the false shipment status was. C misuses temperature, which controls sampling randomness and would make merging less predictable, not more correct. A fixes ordering but not the core defect: even running last, billing would still consume the fabricated 'shipped' default and make the same error — the placeholder, not the sequencing, is the bug.

questions.q: A team lead wants Claude to break a large feature into independent pieces and have a fresh, isolated agent investigate each one (codebase exploration, dependency analysis) in parallel, returning summaries without polluting the main conversation's context. Which Claude Code feature is intended for this?

options.A: Subagents launched via the Task tool, which run independent investigations in isolation and report summaries back to the main agent
options.B: settings.local.json profiles that each spawn their own separate chat window so the work can be split across parallel sessions
options.C: Multiple CLAUDE.md files, one written per feature piece, all loaded into context simultaneously so each area is documented
options.D: A PreToolUse hook configured to fork the current conversation into a separate parallel investigation thread every single time the main agent issues any tool call

questions.explanation: Correct: subagents (via the Task tool) are made for delegating self-contained investigation/work and returning summaries, keeping the main context clean. C is wrong: CLAUDE.md files are static context, not parallel investigating agents. D is wrong: PreToolUse hooks intercept tool calls deterministically; they do not fork conversations into investigations. B is wrong: there is no 'profiles spawn chat windows' feature in settings.local.json; that file holds local config overrides.

questions.q: A finance agent exposes `delete_invoice` whose schema is `{"invoice_id": {"type":"string"}}`. The description says only 'Deletes an invoice.' Audit logs show the model occasionally calls it with `invoice_id` values it guessed or partially constructed, deleting the wrong records, and there is no way to undo. Which set of changes best aligns with safe tool design?

options.A: Broaden the tool into `manage_invoice` covering create, read, and delete in one place, on the theory that more context makes the model guess less
options.B: Make the delete idempotent so that repeating the same id is harmless, then consider the wrong-record problem fully solved by that property alone
options.C: Require a well-formed `invoice_id` pattern in the schema, document that ids must come from a prior lookup rather than being guessed or constructed, and gate this irreversible delete behind an explicit confirmation step
options.D: Return `{"status":"ok"}` on every delete call, including ones where no invoice matched the id, so a missed match never stalls the running agent

questions.explanation: The failure stems from fabricated identifiers feeding an irreversible action. Constraining the id format at validation, instructing that ids must originate from a real lookup rather than being invented, and gating the destructive call behind confirmation together address input quality and irreversibility. B addresses repeat-safety but does nothing about deleting the wrong record the first time. A creates an over-broad 'do everything' tool that widens the privilege surface. D is a silent-success anti-pattern that hides failed or wrong deletes behind a generic 'ok'.

questions.q: A long pipeline prompt is ordered: huge system prompt -> 12 few-shot examples -> user message. You notice extraction accuracy for constraints stated only at the top of the system prompt is markedly worse than for instructions placed right next to the examples. What phenomenon is this, and what is the fix?

options.A: Token budget exhaustion — the large system prompt consumed the tokens that were needed to reason. Fix: delete most of the system prompt to free room.
options.B: Temperature miscalibration — a high temperature is making the model ignore the system prompt entirely. Fix: set the temperature to 0 for the run.
options.C: Instruction conflict — the 12 few-shot examples have silently overridden the system prompt's constraints. Fix: remove all of the examples from the prompt.
options.D: Attention dilution — instructions far from the end of a long context carry less weight. Fix: restate critical constraints near the examples.

questions.explanation: This is attention dilution / lost-in-the-middle: content far from the end of a long context gets less weight. D is correct — relocate or restate the critical constraints near the examples or just before the user message. A misdiagnoses it as token exhaustion. B is the temperature misconception. C wrongly blames the examples and would remove useful guidance.

questions.q: A monorepo has React components, API handlers, and DB repositories, each with distinct conventions. Test files (*.test.tsx) are scattered next to the code they test and must follow one shared testing convention regardless of folder. You want Claude to automatically apply the right conventions based on file path. What is the most maintainable setup?

options.A: Create one skill per code type under .claude/skills/ and rely on Claude choosing to load the matching skill each time it edits.
options.B: Use rule files under .claude/rules/ with glob patterns (e.g. **/*.test.tsx, **/api/**) so conventions attach automatically by path.
options.C: Drop a separate CLAUDE.md into every subdirectory, copying the shared test convention into each folder that contains a test file.
options.D: Put every convention in the root CLAUDE.md under clearly labeled headers and rely on Claude to correctly infer which section applies to the file at hand.

questions.explanation: .claude/rules/ with glob frontmatter applies path-scoped conventions automatically, independent of directory. B is correct. D relies on inference rather than explicit path matching. A relies on Claude choosing to load skills, which isn't automatic by path. C can't cleanly cover test files spread across many directories and duplicates the one shared rule everywhere.

questions.q: A DevOps agent has a single tool `manage_infra` whose description says 'Manage infrastructure: create, resize, reboot, snapshot, or terminate any VM, plus edit firewall rules and DNS.' Mis-selection is low, but a malformed call once terminated a production VM the agent only meant to reboot, and security flags that any task grants the model termination power. What is the best redesign?

options.A: Split it into narrowly scoped tools (reboot_vm, resize_vm, terminate_vm) each with a schema, and confirm the destructive ones
options.B: Keep the single tool but rename it `safe_manage_infra` so the descriptor itself signals caution and nudges the model toward careful use
options.C: Keep the single tool but add a required `confirm: true` field that must be set on every call, regardless of which sub-action is being invoked
options.D: Keep `manage_infra` and write a much longer description that enumerates every sub-action, its arguments, and its blast radius and risks

questions.explanation: A 'do everything' tool violates least privilege and bundles a destructive action (terminate) with benign ones, so every task implicitly grants termination. Splitting into scoped tools lets you grant only what a task needs and confirm specifically the destructive operations. C blanket-confirms even harmless reboots, training model and users to rubber-stamp confirmations. D lengthens the description but still exposes full termination power on every call. B renames without changing the actual privilege surface, which is purely cosmetic.

questions.q: A process_refund tool occasionally receives a malformed amount (e.g., a string '50.00 USD' instead of a number) and silently refunds 0, hiding the failure from the agent. What design change best improves reliability?

options.A: Catch every error inside the tool and always return {"status": "ok"}, so the agent never has to deal with a failure case.
options.B: Move validation into the system prompt by instructing the model to always send a clean numeric amount with no units attached.
options.C: Define a strict input schema (numeric amount, required orderId), reject malformed arguments, and return a structured error.
options.D: Have the tool coerce whatever input arrives into a numeric value however it best can, stripping currency units, and proceed so the refund flow is never interrupted.

questions.explanation: Strict schema validation rejects bad input at the right layer, and structured errors let the agent recover instead of silently succeeding. C is correct. D risks refunding wrong amounts via silent coercion. A is the silent-success anti-pattern that hid the bug in the first place. B relies on probabilistic prompt compliance rather than enforced validation.

questions.q: A nightly pipeline must classify 200,000 archived support emails and store the labels in a data warehouse by the next morning; nothing reads the results in real time. The team wants the lowest cost and is fine with multi-hour turnaround. Which approach fits best?

options.A: Submit all 200,000 emails through the asynchronous Message Batches API, which is about 50% cheaper, and correlate each returned label back to its source email using the per-request custom_id field.
options.B: Concatenate all 200,000 emails into one enormous prompt so the model labels the entire archive in a single call.
options.C: Fire 200,000 synchronous requests in a tight loop to guarantee strict sub-second latency for every single item.
options.D: Use the Batches API but poll it every 100 ms, expecting near-instant results so the pipeline still finishes fast.

questions.explanation: Correct: A — the Batches API is about 50% cheaper, allows up to ~24h turnaround, and correlates results by custom_id, ideal for an overnight non-blocking job. C is wrong: synchronous low-latency calls forgo the batch discount and add no value since nothing reads results in real time. B is wrong: 200,000 emails vastly exceed any context window and would destroy attention and accuracy. D is wrong: Batches has no low-latency SLA, so expecting near-instant results misuses it.

questions.q: While drafting an MCP server, a developer must choose between stdio and HTTP transport. The server runs as a local subprocess spawned by the desktop client on the same machine, with no remote callers. Conceptually, which choice and rationale is most appropriate?

options.A: stdio, because the stdio transport encrypts the channel end-to-end whereas HTTP would transmit the tool arguments in readable plaintext
options.B: HTTP, because a spawned local subprocess has no other channel and must open a loopback TCP port before it can exchange any MCP messages
options.C: stdio, because the desktop client launches the server as a local child process on the same machine and exchanges JSON-RPC messages by writing requests to its standard input and reading responses from its standard output, needing no network endpoint
options.D: HTTP only, because MCP messages can only be framed as HTTP request bodies and stdio has no way to carry structured tool-call payloads

questions.explanation: For a locally spawned subprocess, stdio is the natural transport: the client writes requests to the server's stdin and reads responses from its stdout, with no network endpoint required. D is false; MCP supports multiple transports including stdio, which carries structured messages fine. A invents an encryption property; stdio is chosen for local process communication, not encryption. B is false; a subprocess communicates over its standard streams and need not open a TCP port.

questions.q: A teammate proposes wrapping a trivial task — 'read config.json and tell me the value of the region field' — in a five-subagent orchestration 'to be thorough.' The file path and field are already known. How should you evaluate this?

options.A: Approve it, but first require each of the five subagents to read the entire repository so they share full project context.
options.B: Approve it, since adding more independent subagents reliably increases the final answer's overall quality even on the simplest, fully-specified lookups.
options.C: Reject it purely on cost, then use one subagent that re-reads config.json ten times and votes on the region value for safety.
options.D: Reject the orchestration: a known-file, single-fact lookup should be answered directly, with no decomposition to justify the overhead.

questions.explanation: Multi-agent overhead is justified by breadth, parallelism, or isolation — none present for a single-fact lookup. D is correct. B is false: more agents is not strictly better and adds latency and token cost here. A adds pointless work that doesn't help find one known field. C invents a redundant re-read/voting scheme that adds cost without addressing the real point.

questions.q: A legal-research tool has access to a 500-document corpus totaling millions of tokens. For each user query, the team is deciding whether to prepend the entire corpus to every prompt or to retrieve only the most relevant passages. Which design is the sound default and why?

options.A: Prepend the entire multi-million-token corpus to every prompt so the model can never miss any relevant text.
options.B: Retrieve only the relevant chunks per query, cutting cost, respecting limits, and focusing attention.
options.C: Concatenate the whole corpus once, cache it permanently, and reuse that cache forever at no further token cost.
options.D: Retrieve nothing at query time and rely solely on the model's parametric memory of published legal documents.

questions.explanation: Correct: B — retrieval (RAG) of only relevant chunks keeps cost down, stays within context limits, and focuses attention on what matters, the right default for a huge corpus. A is wrong: millions of tokens exceed the window and dilute attention even where they fit, at high cost. D is wrong: relying on parametric memory for a specific proprietary corpus invites hallucination and misses the actual documents. C is wrong: caching is not permanent and has a short TTL, so 'forever at no cost' is false.

questions.q: A data-engineering team wants Claude Code to run `ruff format` on every Python file the moment it is edited, with zero exceptions, even when the developer forgets to ask. One engineer added a line to CLAUDE.md saying 'Always run ruff format after editing any .py file.' Files still occasionally land unformatted. What is the reliable fix?

options.A: Bold the CLAUDE.md instruction and pin it to the very top of the file so the model consistently prioritizes it over other guidance
options.B: Create a project `/format` slash command wrapping `ruff format` and instruct everyone on the data-engineering team to remember to invoke it manually after each individual file edit they make
options.C: Switch the session into plan mode so Claude always plans and includes the formatting step before performing each file edit
options.D: Add a PostToolUse hook in settings.json running `ruff format`; the harness executes it deterministically whether or not the model remembers

questions.explanation: Correct: deterministic 'always do X after Y' automation is a hook (PostToolUse) in settings.json — the harness runs it, so it cannot be forgotten, and any rationale belongs in docs, not the option. A is wrong: CLAUDE.md relies on the model choosing to comply, which is not reliable for zero-exception guarantees, regardless of formatting or placement. B is wrong: a slash command still depends on a human (or model) remembering to invoke it. C is wrong: plan mode is for planning complex multi-file changes, not for guaranteeing a post-edit action runs every time.

questions.q: A pull-request reviewer is built as an orchestrator that spawns four subagents in parallel: a security scanner, a style checker, a test-coverage analyzer, and a final 'merge-decision' agent that needs all three prior results to decide. Currently all four launch at once and the merge agent frequently decides on incomplete data. How should the dispatch be structured?

options.A: Run security, style, and coverage in parallel, then add a barrier so the merge agent starts only after all three return
options.B: Keep all four launching in parallel but have the merge agent poll the others' partial outputs and proceed once they appear complete
options.C: Run all four subagents strictly one after another in a fixed sequence to guarantee a predictable, well-defined execution ordering
options.D: Merge the four roles into one agent that performs security, style, coverage, and the final decision sequentially in a single shared context

questions.explanation: The first three tasks are independent and the fourth has a true data dependency on all of them — the textbook case for parallel fan-out plus a barrier. A is correct: it maximizes concurrency for the independent work while ensuring the dependent step waits for complete inputs. C is needlessly slow: it serializes three tasks that have no dependency on each other, wasting the parallelism. B reintroduces the original bug — guessing completion is non-deterministic and is what caused decisions on incomplete data. D throws away the isolation and parallelism benefits and crams unrelated concerns into one context, increasing error and token pressure without solving the dependency-ordering issue cleanly.

questions.q: A security-conscious team configures a PreToolUse hook in settings.json that inspects proposed Bash commands and blocks any matching `rm -rf` or writes outside the repo, returning an error to Claude. A developer assumes that because Claude 'decides' what to run, a hook can't actually stop a tool call. What is correct about hooks and the permission flow?

options.A: Hooks merely surface advisory text alongside the proposed command, which the model is then free to read, weigh, and ultimately ignore
options.B: Hooks can only ever fire after a given tool has already finished running, so they are able to log a dangerous command afterward for later human review but can never actually prevent it
options.C: PreToolUse hooks run in the harness before the tool executes and can deny the call, deterministically blocking dangerous commands whatever the model intended to run
options.D: Only the IDE integration is able to block tool calls; hooks declared in settings.json carry no real enforcement power when running in the CLI

questions.explanation: Correct: PreToolUse hooks execute in the harness before a tool runs and can intercept and deny the call, giving deterministic enforcement independent of the model's intent. B is wrong: that describes PostToolUse; PreToolUse runs before execution and can prevent it. A is wrong: hooks are harness-executed automation with real enforcement, not advisory text the model may ignore. D is wrong: settings.json hooks enforce in the CLI as well, not only in an IDE.

questions.q: An orchestrator collects outputs from three subagents that researched a vendor: one returns a glowing security assessment, one returns a critical one citing a recent breach, and one returns 'insufficient data.' The current synthesis step naively concatenates all three into the final answer, producing a self-contradictory report. What is the best synthesis approach?

options.A: Have synthesis select whichever subagent produced the most text for its assessment, since greater length is a reasonable proxy for analytical thoroughness
options.B: Have synthesis reconcile the findings: weigh the evidence, surface and explain the disagreement, and produce one coherent assessment noting the data gap
options.C: Have synthesis average the three assessments into a single neutral verdict so the final report avoids taking sides on the conflicting security findings
options.D: Re-run all three subagents repeatedly until their assessments converge on agreement, then concatenate the now-consistent outputs into the final report

questions.explanation: Synthesis means reconciling and integrating worker outputs into a coherent whole, especially when they conflict. B is correct: a good synthesizer weighs the evidence (a cited recent breach is material), explicitly surfaces the disagreement, and flags the data gap rather than hiding it. A uses length as a proxy for quality, which is unfounded and could elevate the wrong conclusion. C blends contradictory signals into a mushy 'neutral' verdict that buries a real security concern. D assumes consensus is achievable and forces re-runs until agreement, which can loop indefinitely and pressures workers toward false agreement instead of honest synthesis of genuine conflict.

questions.q: A team is debating what the Model Context Protocol (MCP) actually standardizes before adopting it across three internal agents. Which statement is correct?

options.A: MCP is a quantized weight-exchange format that lets one model's trained tool-use head be loaded and reused by a different model
options.B: MCP is a payload compression scheme that shrinks large tool-call arguments and results on the wire to cut token and bandwidth cost
options.C: MCP is a replacement for HTTPS that supplies its own transport-layer encryption and certificate handling between client and server
options.D: MCP standardizes how an application interoperably exposes tools, resources, and prompts so clients discover and call them without per-integration glue

questions.explanation: MCP is an open protocol standardizing the interoperable exposure of tools, resources, and prompts so any compliant client can discover and invoke them. A is wrong: MCP has nothing to do with model weights or quantization. B is wrong: it defines message structure, not byte-level compression. C is wrong: MCP is not a security/transport encryption layer and does not replace HTTPS; it can run over transports like stdio or HTTP.

questions.q: You expose an MCP server with two tools whose descriptions are 'Get user' and 'Get account'. The agent constantly picks the wrong one, mixing up userId and accountId, which corrupts downstream calls with financial impact. What is the most effective FIRST step?

options.A: Merge the two tools into a single get_entity tool that inspects the identifier passed in and decides internally which lookup to run.
options.B: Expand both descriptions to state what each returns, the identifier format expected, examples, and when to use one over the other.
options.C: Add a sentence to the system prompt telling the model to be careful and to make sure it always picks the right tool every time.
options.D: Put a routing classifier in front of the model that parses each incoming user message and pre-selects the tool by matching keywords.

questions.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism for selection, and minimal ones ('Get user'/'Get account') are the root cause. B is the low-effort, high-leverage first step. D is over-engineered as a first move and brittle to phrasing. A is a heavier architectural change than a first step warrants and hides the ambiguity rather than resolving it. C is vague and gives the model no new distinguishing information.

questions.q: A frontend guild packages reusable, multi-step expertise — how to scaffold a new component with their design-system tokens, tests, and Storybook story following house conventions — so Claude can invoke that capability on demand with its bundled instructions and helper files. Which Claude Code mechanism is purpose-built for packaging such reusable capabilities?

options.A: A line in the root CLAUDE.md instructing Claude to 'know how to scaffold components with our design-system tokens and tests'
options.B: A PreToolUse hook that intercepts and blocks any edit to component or Storybook story files unless the full scaffolding sequence has demonstrably already been run first
options.C: A skill, which bundles instructions and supporting resources for a reusable capability Claude invokes on demand when it is relevant
options.D: settings.json permission entries that allowlist the scaffolding shell command so Claude is authorized to execute it directly

questions.explanation: Correct: skills package reusable, multi-step capabilities with their own instructions and bundled resources that Claude can invoke when relevant — exactly this scaffolding workflow. A is wrong: a vague CLAUDE.md sentence is unstructured context, not a packaged, invokable capability with bundled files. B is wrong: a PreToolUse hook is a deterministic gate/automation, not a way to package expertise. D is wrong: an allowlist permission merely authorizes a command to run; it carries none of the procedural knowledge or resources a skill bundles.

questions.q: Your orchestrator delegates a 'find every place we call the deprecated chargeCard() API' task to a search subagent. The subagent works, but the orchestrator's context fills up almost immediately and the run aborts. Inspecting the transcript, you see the subagent returned the full contents of all 60 files it opened, not just the matches. What change most directly fixes the reliability problem?

options.A: Have the subagent return only matched file paths, line numbers, and call sites — its conclusion, not the raw files.
options.B: Drop the delegation and run the file search inline in the orchestrator itself, so there is no inter-agent payload transfer at all.
options.C: Lower the subagent's temperature so it produces shorter, more focused responses with much less surrounding explanatory prose.
options.D: Raise the orchestrator's max output tokens so it can absorb the larger inbound payload from the search subagent.

questions.explanation: Subagents should return distilled conclusions, not raw file dumps, to protect the orchestrator's context. A is correct: returning only matched call sites keeps the payload tiny and useful. D treats the symptom and just delays the overflow while raising cost — output tokens don't bound what the subagent sends back anyway. B throws away the isolation/parallelism benefit of delegation and would fill the orchestrator's own context with the same 60 files. C conflates randomness with verbosity — temperature doesn't control whether the subagent dumps whole files.

questions.q: A product-review analyzer must output {category, priority}. The team wired priority to be 'high' whenever the review's sentiment is strongly negative. They're puzzled that an angry one-star review reading 'I hate the new icon color' is flagged high priority while a calm note 'app silently loses my unsaved work' is flagged low. What is the core mistake?

options.A: The sampling temperature is set far too high, so the sentiment scoring comes out noisy and inconsistent across reviews and should be pinned to exactly 0 for stable, repeatable priorities
options.B: Conflating sentiment with severity — priority needs its own decomposed criteria (data loss, security, blocked workflow), not emotional tone
options.C: The prompt simply lacks enough few-shot examples of angry, strongly negative reviews to learn how to assign priority correctly to them
options.D: The model should emit a self-reported confidence score on its sentiment so that low-confidence reviews can be downranked in priority

questions.explanation: Correct (B): sentiment is not the same as severity or complexity. Priority should be driven by explicit decomposed criteria — data loss, security, blocked core workflow — not emotional tone, which is exactly why the ranking inverts. A misattributes a definitional flaw to randomness; temperature=0 won't make tone equal severity. C adds examples of the wrong signal (anger), reinforcing the mistaken mapping. D leans on poorly calibrated self-reported confidence and still keeps sentiment as the basis for priority.

questions.q: During a long multi-step agent run, one subtask involves fetching and reasoning over a noisy 15,000-token debug log that is irrelevant to later subtasks. An engineer worries that keeping this log in the shared context for the rest of the run will hurt later steps. What is the best practice?

options.A: Scope the noisy 15,000-token log strictly to the single subtask that needs it, then forward only a brief distilled result to the later steps, keeping the shared context clean.
options.B: Keep the full raw log resident in context for the whole run so every later subtask has maximum information.
options.C: Re-paste the entire raw debug log before each subsequent subtask to guarantee consistency across the steps.
options.D: Raise the model's context window limit for this run so the noisy log never needs to be removed at all.

questions.explanation: Correct: A — avoiding context pollution means confining irrelevant data to the subtask that needs it and forwarding only a concise result, which preserves attention and cost across later steps. B is wrong: carrying a noisy irrelevant log forward pollutes context and wastes tokens. C is wrong: re-pasting it repeatedly multiplies token cost and worsens the pollution. D is wrong: the context window limit is fixed and cannot simply be raised on demand.

questions.q: A reporting agent exposes `run_query` with input `{"sql": {"type":"string"}}` and the description 'Run a query.' Telemetry shows the model frequently passes natural-language strings ('top customers last month') instead of SQL, and also occasionally passes DROP/DELETE statements. Which combination of fixes is the soundest first step?

options.A: Silently parse any natural-language input into the equivalent SQL inside the handler and execute the generated statement without surfacing it
options.B: Keep the current schema unchanged and attach a post-hoc audit log that records every statement executed so misuse can be reviewed afterward
options.C: Add a second tool `run_natural_language_query` alongside the SQL one and route between them with a classifier that inspects each incoming request
options.D: State in the description that it expects a read-only SELECT, with an example, and have the handler reject non-SELECT input

questions.explanation: The description fails to specify the required input format (SQL, read-only), which drives the model to send natural language, and there is no validation against destructive statements. Clarifying the expected format with an example plus rejecting non-SELECT input with a structured error fixes both at the right layer. C introduces a routing classifier and a new tool, over-engineered as a first move. A silently transforms input, hiding behavior and risking incorrect queries run without the agent's awareness. B only records damage after the fact and prevents nothing.

questions.q: A customer-support assistant runs a single chat session for an entire 8-hour shift, accumulating hundreds of turns. As the conversation approaches the model's context window limit, the agent harness performs compaction. Which statement best describes what happens to the session afterward?

options.A: It silently discards the newest messages to free room, retaining only the very oldest turns of the chat.
options.B: The whole session is force-restarted from scratch, dropping all prior knowledge so the user re-explains everything.
options.C: It condenses the older earlier turns into a compact summary and carries that summary plus the most recent context forward, so the agent keeps working without losing the thread.
options.D: The physical context window is temporarily expanded to fit every accumulated turn until the shift finally ends.

questions.explanation: Correct: C — compaction summarizes earlier content and carries a summary plus the remaining recent context forward, so the session keeps going without a restart and the user need not re-explain. D is wrong: the physical token limit is fixed and is not raised or expanded by compaction. A is wrong: compaction keeps recent context and summarizes the old material, rather than dropping the newest messages. B is wrong: compaction exists precisely to avoid a from-scratch restart that would discard prior knowledge.

questions.q: Your team requires that prettier runs on every file Claude Code edits, with zero exceptions, before the change is considered done. Developers keep forgetting to ask for it and unformatted code slips into PRs. What is the most reliable mechanism?

options.A: Create a shared /format slash command for the team and rely on each developer to remember to invoke it manually after every file they have Claude edit.
options.B: Save a feedback-type memory entry reminding Claude to format files, so the reminder persists and resurfaces across future sessions.
options.C: Add an 'always run prettier after editing' instruction to CLAUDE.md and trust the model to remember and comply on every single edit.
options.D: Add a PostToolUse hook in settings.json that runs the formatter after each edit, since the harness executes hooks deterministically.

questions.explanation: Deterministic 'always do X after Y' automation belongs in a hook executed by the harness, not the model. D is correct. C and B both rely on the model remembering, which can't deliver a zero-exception guarantee. A depends on a human invoking the command every time, the exact failure mode described.

questions.q: A medical-records pipeline extracts {patient_id, diagnosis_code, visit_date} from clinical notes. The prompt ends with 'Return only JSON, no other text.' but about 9% of responses still wrap the JSON in prose, include trailing commentary, or emit malformed objects, and these crash the downstream parser. The team wants the highest-reliability fix for production. What should they do?

options.A: Set temperature to 0 so the decoding becomes deterministic and the model reliably emits clean, parser-safe JSON
options.B: Repeat the sentence 'Return only JSON, no other text.' three more times at the start, middle, and end of the prompt for emphasis
options.C: Define the three typed fields as a tool / structured-output schema and validate every response at the tool layer, retrying on failure
options.D: Post-process each response with a brittle regex that grabs the substring spanning from the very first '{' to the very last '}' character and silently discards everything outside that range

questions.explanation: Correct (C): the most reliable production pattern is forced structured output via a tool/JSON Schema, validated at the tool layer with retry on mismatch — the model is constrained to the schema rather than asked politely to comply, and the extra justification belongs here in the explanation, not the option. B just restates an instruction the model already sometimes ignores and adds tokens without enforcing structure. A is a common misconception: temperature=0 reduces randomness but does not enforce a grammar or fix instruction-following, so non-conforming completions still occur. D is brittle salvage — a regex cannot recover malformed or semantically wrong objects and silently mangles nested or escaped content.

questions.q: A CI job runs Claude Code to auto-generate a changelog from merged commits and write it to a file. A developer copies their interactive habits into the pipeline and the job hangs forever waiting for input. A teammate claims the fix is to set `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=1` and pass `--batch`. What is actually correct?

options.A: Run `claude -p "..."` (equivalently `--print`), the non-interactive headless mode that takes the prompt as an argument and exits
options.B: Pass the `--batch` flag so the pipeline can queue several prompts in sequence and run them without ever attaching to a TTY
options.C: Set the `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=1` environment variable, the documented way to suppress the interactive prompt when Claude Code runs inside a CI pipeline
options.D: Pipe `yes |` into the interactive `claude` session so that every single permission prompt the running job encounters is automatically confirmed for you

questions.explanation: Correct: `claude -p` (alias `--print`) is the non-interactive/headless invocation for CI — it takes the prompt and runs to completion without an interactive session. C is wrong: there is no `CLAUDE_HEADLESS` environment variable; it is a classic distractor. B is wrong: there is no `--batch` flag. D is wrong: auto-confirming an interactive session with `yes` is a fragile hack and not how headless mode is meant to be run; it also doesn't supply the missing `-p` prompt argument.

questions.q: A real-time coding assistant must return autocomplete suggestions within a 300 ms budget while the developer types. A teammate proposes routing these suggestions through the Message Batches API to cut inference cost by roughly half. Why is this a poor fit?

options.A: The Batches API actually costs more per token than synchronous calls, so there is no cost benefit here to weigh.
options.B: Batches offers no low-latency SLA and can take up to ~24h, unfit for a 300 ms interactive budget.
options.C: The Batches API only accepts text-classification jobs and is technically unable to generate any code completions.
options.D: The Batches API cannot correlate results back to individual requests, so the returned suggestions would be misattributed.

questions.explanation: Correct: B — Batches has no latency guarantee and may take many hours, so it cannot serve a 300 ms interactive autocomplete need despite the ~50% discount. A is wrong: Batches is about 50% cheaper, not more expensive. D is wrong: Batches correlates results via custom_id, so attribution is not the problem. C is wrong: Batches is not restricted to classification; the disqualifier is latency, not task type.

questions.q: A coordinator launches a code-search subagent and a dependency-graph subagent in parallel. The code-search subagent crashes with a tool exception mid-run. It returns the literal Python traceback string to the coordinator, which inserts the traceback into its next prompt and then hallucinates a 'fixed' file path that doesn't exist. What is the better error-propagation design?

options.A: Have the subagent return the raw Python traceback verbatim to the coordinator, on the principle that maximum diagnostic detail gives it the most to work with
options.B: Have the coordinator immediately abort the entire run, including the parallel dependency-graph subagent, the moment any subagent throws an unhandled exception
options.C: Have the subagent swallow the exception silently and return an empty success result so the parallel pipeline keeps moving without stalling on the failure
options.D: Have the subagent catch the failure and return a structured error object: the operation, the failed tool, work completed, and whether retry is safe

questions.explanation: Coordinators recover best from structured error context, not raw stack traces or silence. D is correct: a typed error describing the failed operation, partial progress, and retry-safety lets the coordinator retry, route around, or report cleanly. A dumps an unstructured traceback into the prompt, which is what led the coordinator to hallucinate — raw traces are noisy context, not actionable signal. C is silent-success, hiding the failure and corrupting downstream results with a fake empty answer. B over-reacts: aborting the whole run on any single failure throws away the parallel dependency-graph work that may have succeeded and prevents graceful degradation.

questions.q: A deployment agent's workflow must run run_tests() then, only if tests pass, call deploy(). The team implemented this purely via the prompt: 'Run the tests; if they pass, deploy.' Metrics show that under long, busy conversations the model occasionally deploys after a partial or failed test run because it misreads the test output. Deploys to production are irreversible within the window. What is the correct enforcement?

options.A: Ask the model to explicitly restate the run_tests() results in its own words immediately before deploying so it reads the test output more carefully
options.B: Add a reviewer subagent that reads the full conversation after each deploy attempt and decides whether the deployment looked justified given the test output
options.C: Gate deploy() on a machine-checked test artifact such as an exit code or signed pass token; deploy() refuses to run unless that artifact says pass
options.D: Move the conditional deploy instruction to the very top of the system prompt and repeat it again at the end so the model is far more likely to honor it

questions.explanation: An irreversible production action conditioned on a pass/fail fact must be enforced by checking a machine-verifiable artifact, not by the model's reading of text. C is correct: gating deploy() on an exit code or signed token removes the model's interpretation from the critical path — it physically cannot deploy on a failed/partial run. D and A both rely on the model correctly interpreting test output, the exact failure observed under long contexts; placement and restatement reduce but do not eliminate misreads. B is reactive and itself probabilistic — the reviewer subagent can also misjudge, and by the time it reviews, the irreversible deploy may already be done.

questions.q: A logistics agent has two tools: `cancel_shipment` (voids a not-yet-dispatched shipment) and `recall_shipment` (issues a return-to-sender on an in-transit shipment). Dashboards show the model picks `cancel_shipment` for in-transit packages 38% of the time, causing failed cancellations. Both descriptions read only 'Stops a shipment.' What is the most effective FIRST step?

options.A: Add a five-example few-shot block to the system prompt contrasting cancel-vs-recall cases the model can pattern-match against
options.B: Expand each description to state the shipment state it applies to (pre-dispatch vs in-transit) and a when-not-to-use boundary line
options.C: Insert an LLM routing classifier ahead of the toolset that pre-decides cancel vs recall before either tool is ever exposed
options.D: Merge the two tools into one `stop_shipment` that inspects the shipment state internally and branches to the correct void or recall path

questions.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary signal the model uses to select tools, and these are nearly empty, so expanding them with the distinguishing shipment state plus a when-not-to-use boundary is the low-effort, high-leverage first fix; extra justification belongs here, not crammed into the option. D (merging) discards a meaningful least-privilege boundary and is over-engineered as a first move. A (few-shot) adds tokens and is more brittle than fixing the descriptions the model actually reads at selection time. C (routing classifier) bolts on a whole component and latency to compensate for descriptions that were never written properly.

questions.q: An agent reads a 25,000-token specification file at the start of a task. Forty turns later it needs a detail from that spec and chooses to re-read the entire file from disk rather than relying on what's already in context. From a token-budget perspective, what is the key consideration?

options.A: The full re-read re-injects all 25,000 tokens, adding cost; reusing context or reading only the needed section is cheaper.
options.B: Re-reading the file from disk is effectively free because file reads never consume any of the context token budget.
options.C: Re-reading actually reduces total token usage because the new copy replaces the earlier one at no additional cost.
options.D: Re-reading is always required here because content already in context is automatically purged after a few turns.

questions.explanation: Correct: A — re-reading the whole file pushes all 25,000 tokens back into context, costing tokens and budget; targeting only the needed section or reusing existing context is cheaper. B is wrong: content read into context counts against the token budget. D is wrong: prior context is not automatically purged every few turns, so a full re-read is not mandatory. C is wrong: re-reading adds tokens rather than replacing the earlier copy for free.

questions.q: An orchestrator dispatches one subagent per microservice to summarize that service's recent error logs. Each subagent returns its full raw log file (8k–20k tokens) plus a one-line summary. After 11 subagents the orchestrator's context is 94% full and it begins truncating earlier summaries before it can produce the cross-service report. What is the best design change?

options.A: Run the 11 subagents strictly sequentially so only one raw log file occupies the orchestrator's context at any given moment
options.B: Increase the orchestrator's maximum context window so that all 11 full raw log files plus their one-line summaries fit without any truncation during cross-service synthesis
options.C: Have each subagent return only its distilled conclusion — top error patterns, counts, suspected root cause — not the raw logs
options.D: Have the orchestrator collect all 11 raw logs first and then re-summarize each one itself before writing the cross-service report

questions.explanation: Subagents exist partly to protect the orchestrator's context by returning distilled conclusions, not raw dumps. C is correct: compact synthesized findings let the orchestrator hold all 11 results and reason across them. B treats the symptom — a bigger window still fills with low-value raw text and costs more; the raw logs add no value at the orchestrator level. A does not help because the orchestrator must retain all summaries simultaneously to write a cross-service report; sequencing the workers does not shrink what must coexist at synthesis time. D is wasteful and self-defeating: it pulls every raw log into the orchestrator (the exact overflow) and duplicates work the subagents should have done.

questions.q: An order-fulfillment agent must, for refunds over $500, always call manager_approval() before issue_refund(). Product wants this guaranteed even as prompts evolve across releases. A proposal suggests adding a clear instruction and a few-shot example to the system prompt. Over a 50k-request canary, 7 refunds over $500 were issued without approval. What should ship to production?

options.A: Routing every refund request through a dedicated refund subagent that carries the same approval instruction and few-shot example in its own system prompt
options.B: A nightly evaluator subagent that audits the day's refunds and emails the finance team about any over-$500 refund that was issued without approval
options.C: A more emphatic system prompt stating the approval rule plus several few-shot examples of the over-$500 flow
options.D: A programmatic guard inside issue_refund() itself that refuses to process any refund above $500 unless a valid, unexpired approval token issued by manager_approval() is present and verified, making the unapproved over-$500 path structurally impossible regardless of how the surrounding prompts drift across future releases

questions.explanation: D hard business rule on a money-moving action must be enforced deterministically, not by prompt compliance. D is correct: making issue_refund() itself require a valid approval token closes the gap regardless of prompt drift — the unsafe path becomes impossible. C is the approach that already let 7 violations through; more emphasis lowers but never zeroes a probabilistic rate. B catches violations after the money has left, which is too late for a control that must prevent the action. A just relocates the same fallible prompt instruction into a subagent; the enforcement layer is still probabilistic and will still leak.

questions.q: A team adds eight few-shot examples to an address-normalization prompt, but the failures persist: the model still can't handle apartment-suffix formats it has never been shown, and per-request cost has climbed. Investigation shows the underlying instruction never defines how to format apartment suffixes at all. What is the best conclusion?

options.A: Remove the natural-language instruction text entirely and rely purely on the few-shot examples to teach the model how to normalize addresses
options.B: Examples don't substitute for a missing rule: define the apartment-suffix format explicitly, keep a couple of illustrative examples, and stop adding more
options.C: Eight few-shot examples is simply far too few for the model to generalize, so the right move is to scale up to roughly 50 hand-curated examples covering every known apartment-suffix format
options.D: Switch the decoding to temperature=0 so that the eight existing few-shot examples are applied deterministically and consistently to every new address

questions.explanation: Correct (B): when the root cause is an undefined rule, more few-shot examples add token overhead without fixing it; you state the rule explicitly and use a couple of examples to illustrate. C keeps treating a missing-specification problem as a coverage problem, ballooning cost and still missing unseen formats. D confuses determinism with having a rule to apply. A discards explicit instruction in favor of inference-by-example, which is exactly the weak strategy that already failed.

questions.q: An agent calls a `charge_card` tool. Because of a network timeout the client retries the same call three times with identical arguments, and metrics show some customers were charged twice. The tool currently accepts `{amount, currency, customer_id}` and processes every call. What design change most directly prevents the double charges?

options.A: Require a caller-supplied `idempotency_key` on every charge so the server records the key, treats any retry carrying the same key as the one original operation, and returns the original result instead of charging again
options.B: Add a human confirmation step before every single charge so an operator can review the amount and catch any duplicate request manually before money actually moves
options.C: Wrap each charge in a try/catch, detect duplicates server-side, and return `{"status":"ok"}` for the duplicate so the agent keeps moving
options.D: Lower the client's automatic retry count from three to one so the tool is simply invoked fewer times under transient network errors

questions.explanation: Idempotency is the correct property for a destructive financial action: an idempotency key lets the server recognize a retried request as the same operation and avoid charging twice while still returning a coherent result. D reduces but does not eliminate the race and abandons resilience to transient failures. C hides duplicate detection behind a generic success, a silent-success anti-pattern that may already have double-charged. B adds human friction to every charge and does not make the operation safe under automated retries.

questions.q: A team builds a prompt with three parts: a 20,000-token policy manual (identical across all users), a 4,000-token tenant-specific configuration (changes per customer), and a short per-request user message. They want to maximize prompt-cache hit rates across requests from the same tenant. How should they order the content?

options.A: Tenant configuration first, then the per-request user message, then the shared policy manual at the end.
options.B: Place the shared 20,000-token policy manual first, then the per-tenant configuration block, then the variable per-request user message last, so the longest stable prefix stays reusable.
options.C: Interleave the user message between the policy manual and the tenant configuration to keep the blocks balanced.
options.D: User message first, then the tenant configuration, then the shared policy manual placed at the very end.

questions.explanation: Correct: B — caching reuses processed prefixes, so the most stable content (the shared policy manual) goes first, then the moderately stable tenant config, then the variable user message last, maximizing reusable prefix length. D is wrong: putting the variable user message first invalidates the prefix on every request, so nothing downstream can be cached. A is wrong: placing the volatile user message before the large stable manual breaks the cacheable prefix. C is wrong: interleaving variable content between stable blocks fragments the prefix and prevents caching the manual.

questions.q: A team enabled prompt caching for a shared 18,000-token system preamble and expected steady savings, but their dashboards show inconsistent, often low cache hit rates. Investigation reveals requests are sporadic — sometimes minutes apart — and that an auto-generated timestamp string is injected at the very top of the preamble on each call. What two factors best explain the poor hit rate?

options.A: The cache stores final responses, so two differing questions can never reuse it regardless of how content is ordered.
options.B: The preamble is far too small to be eligible for caching, and a cache entry never expires once it has been written.
options.C: The auto-generated leading timestamp changes the very start of the prefix and invalidates it on every call, while the long sporadic idle gaps let the short-TTL cache entries expire before they can be reused.
options.D: Caching only ever takes effect at temperature 0, and these particular requests all ran at a higher temperature.

questions.explanation: Correct: C — placing a changing timestamp first breaks the stable prefix so the cache cannot match, and sporadic traffic exceeds the short (~few-minute) TTL so entries expire before reuse. D is wrong: caching does not require temperature 0. A is wrong: caching reuses processed prompt prefixes, not stored final responses, and a stable prefix would help across differing questions. B is wrong: the issue is not size, and the cache does expire via its short TTL rather than persisting forever.

questions.q: A research orchestrator decomposes 'analyze our top 3 competitors' pricing, hiring, and product velocity' into subagents. The current decomposition creates one subagent per competitor, and each is told to cover all three dimensions. Results are inconsistent: one subagent deeply covered pricing but skipped hiring, another did the reverse. The synthesis step has lopsided coverage. How should the decomposition be improved?

options.A: Collapse to a single subagent that handles all three competitors across all three dimensions in one pass to keep coverage perfectly consistent
options.B: Spawn nine subagents, one per competitor-dimension cell, so each narrow slice is researched independently regardless of cost or overlap between them
options.C: Add a fourth 'coverage' subagent that reads the three competitor reports and fills in any missing dimensions from its own prior knowledge
options.D: Give each subagent an explicit bounded scope with a checklist of the exact dimensions and output fields it must return, so coverage is uniform

questions.explanation: Task-decomposition quality depends on giving each worker a clear, bounded scope and a defined output contract. D is correct: an explicit checklist of dimensions and required fields produces uniform, mergeable results and eliminates the lopsided coverage. C papers over the gap by inventing missing analysis from a model's prior knowledge rather than actual research — unreliable and possibly fabricated. A removes parallelism and overloads one context with nine cells, the original under-specification problem at larger scale. B is over-engineering on the other extreme: nine subagents may be defensible only if each cell is genuinely heavy, but here it multiplies orchestration cost and synthesis complexity when a clear per-competitor contract already fixes the issue.

questions.q: An architect is asked to migrate a 40-table SQLAlchemy schema to a new multi-tenant design touching dozens of models, migrations, and query layers, where several valid sequencing strategies exist and a wrong order risks data-integrity bugs. The architect wants Claude to lay out the approach for review before any file is changed. Which Claude Code capability is the best fit?

options.A: Plan mode, which produces a structured plan for a large multi-file task with multiple valid approaches before any edit is made
options.B: A `.claude/rules/` glob that auto-applies the migration's naming and typing conventions to each model file as it is edited
options.C: Headless `claude -p` invoked once so the entire 40-table multi-tenant migration, including every model and query-layer rewrite, runs unattended end to end in a single pass
options.D: A PostToolUse hook that automatically documents and records each schema change after the corresponding migration file has already been written to disk

questions.explanation: Correct: plan mode is designed for complex, large-scale, architectural tasks with multiple valid approaches — it lets Claude present a plan for review before editing. D is wrong: a PostToolUse hook reacts after changes are made, which is the opposite of planning before editing. C is wrong: running such a risky multi-strategy migration unattended in headless mode removes the human review the architect explicitly wants. B is wrong: rules inject per-file conventions but do not produce an up-front migration strategy for review.

questions.q: An orchestrator fans out five subagents to gather data for a quarterly report. Subagent 3 (regional sales for APAC) times out after 60s having retrieved 2 of 4 countries. It returns nothing, and the orchestrator silently proceeds, producing a report that omits APAC entirely with no warning. What is the best handling pattern?

options.A: Have the orchestrator treat any non-response as a success and continue, on the assumption that most of the required data is usually present anyway
options.B: On timeout, have the subagent return a generic 'error' string so the orchestrator at least knows that something went wrong during retrieval
options.C: Increase every subagent's timeout to 10 minutes so that slow data sources finish in time and timeouts effectively never occur in practice
options.D: On timeout, have the subagent return a structured partial result: the 2 countries it got plus a flag listing the 2 missing

questions.explanation: Partial results plus structured context let the coordinator recover intelligently. D is correct: returning the data it did obtain along with an explicit list of what is missing lets the orchestrator retry just the gap, annotate the report, or degrade transparently. B is too coarse — a generic error tells the orchestrator nothing about what was retrieved or what to retry, so the recoverable partial data is lost. A is the actual bug: silent success on missing data is how APAC vanished without warning. C pretends the failure mode can be configured away; long timeouts increase latency for everyone and still do not handle the case where a source is genuinely unavailable.

questions.q: A document-processing agent loops: it calls extract_page() then check_done(), repeating until check_done() returns true. On one malformed 3-page PDF, check_done() keeps returning false because page 3 never parses, and the loop has run 240 iterations re-extracting the same page, burning tokens and money. What is the most appropriate fix?

options.A: Add a programmatic guard that caps iterations and exits with a structured 'failed to converge' result when extraction stops progressing
options.B: Replace the iterative loop with a single mega-prompt that asks the model to extract the full text content of every page in the malformed PDF in one combined pass
options.C: Instruct the agent in its prompt to monitor its own behavior and stop as soon as it notices it is repeating the same extraction step
options.D: Add a second supervisor subagent whose sole job is to watch the first agent's iterations and instruct it to stop when stuck

questions.explanation: Agent loops need a deterministic termination condition independent of model judgment. A is correct: a hard iteration cap plus a no-progress detector guarantees the loop ends and returns a structured failure the caller can handle. C relies on the model noticing its own repetition — unreliable, and it is precisely the kind of probabilistic self-monitoring that already failed implicitly. B changes the extraction strategy and may help throughput but does not provide a guaranteed stop condition, and a malformed page can still derail a single call without a bound. D adds a whole agent to do what one counter does, classic over-engineering, and the watcher itself relies on probabilistic judgment to decide when to intervene.

questions.q: An agent built around a tool-use loop is supposed to keep calling tools until a task is done, then stop and answer. In production it sometimes loops forever, re-calling the same list_files tool with identical arguments dozens of times before timing out. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Switch to a larger-context model so the loop has far more room to keep running before it exhausts the window and times out.
options.B: Remove the list_files tool entirely so the agent has no way to call it repeatedly and the loop cannot stall on it.
options.C: Give the agent an explicit stop condition, treat a repeated identical call as 'already have it,' and cap iterations.
options.D: Raise the temperature on each iteration so the agent varies its behavior and eventually breaks out of the repeated call pattern.

questions.explanation: Agent loops need a clear termination condition plus a deterministic backstop. C is correct: defining when to stop and capping iterations guarantees the loop ends. B removes a needed capability to mask a control-flow bug. D adds randomness, which is not a reliable termination mechanism — the loop can still repeat. A just lets the runaway loop burn more tokens before failing the same way.

questions.q: A team is deciding whether to convert a single-agent invoice processor into a multi-agent system. The task is: read one PDF invoice, extract 6 fields, and write them to a database — done thousands of times a day, each invoice independent and self-contained. A junior engineer proposes a coordinator with extractor, validator, and writer subagents per invoice. What is the soundest recommendation?

options.A: Adopt the three-subagent design and also parallelize across invoices, with one coordinator managing all in-flight invoices together in a shared context
options.B: Keep it as a single agent, or an equivalent deterministic per-invoice pipeline, and scale horizontally by running many independent instances; the narrow, self-contained extract-six-fields-and-write task gains nothing from multi-agent coordination and would only pay extra latency and token cost for the orchestration
options.C: Run an evaluator-optimizer loop on every invoice that iteratively refines the six extracted fields until the extraction reaches near-perfect accuracy
options.D: Adopt the three-subagent extractor-validator-writer design, since separating each concern reliably improves correctness

questions.explanation: Multi-agent overhead is justified by breadth, heavy parallel context needs, or hard isolation requirements — none present here. B is correct: a narrow, self-contained per-invoice task is best served by one agent or a plain deterministic pipeline; you can still scale by running many independent instances. D treats 'separation of concerns' as a universal good, but for a trivial extraction it just multiplies model calls and coordination latency. A compounds the over-engineering and adds a shared-context coordinator that would bloat context across thousands of invoices. C applies an iterative refinement loop to a deterministic extraction problem, adding rounds of cost for a task that does not need open-ended optimization.

questions.q: An MCP server exposes `apply_promo_code` with input schema `{"code": {"type":"string"}}` and no other constraints. Production logs show calls with `code: ""`, `code: "  SAVE10 "`, and `code: null` slipping through; the handler then matches no promo and returns `{"status":"ok","discount":0}`. Which change best addresses the root problem?

options.A: Tighten the schema (minLength, pattern, required) and return a structured `{"error":"promo_not_found"}` instead of a zero-discount success
options.B: Leave the schema as-is but emit a warning log on every zero-discount result so analysts can review malformed codes after the fact
options.C: Trim and uppercase the code inside the handler before lookup, then still return `{"status":"ok","discount":0}` whenever nothing matches
options.D: Change the return to a bare `{"status":"ok"}` and drop the discount field entirely so the model stops over-trusting a zero value

questions.explanation: Two defects compound: a loose schema lets malformed input through, and the tool silently succeeds on failure. Tightening the schema rejects bad input at the validation layer and a structured error makes the failure machine-readable so the agent can react. B only adds passive logging and never stops the silent success. C fixes input hygiene but still reports success when the promo does not exist, masking the failure. D removes information and still signals 'ok' on a failed lookup, the silent-success anti-pattern.

questions.q: A backend team keeps a Postgres connection string and a staging API token in their project's CLAUDE.md so Claude 'always has the credentials it needs' when writing integration code. During onboarding a new hire clones the repo and the secrets are now on their laptop. A senior engineer flags this in review. What is the correct understanding of CLAUDE.md here?

options.A: CLAUDE.md is encrypted at rest by Claude Code, so storing the token there stays safe as long as the repository remains private and access-controlled to the team
options.B: Moving the secrets into a settings.local.json block embedded in CLAUDE.md's frontmatter keeps them out of context while still loading them at runtime
options.C: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded, version-controlled context, so it must never hold secrets; reference credentials by name and inject them at runtime
options.D: Secrets in CLAUDE.md load only when a slash command explicitly pulls them in, so the new hire was never actually exposed to the values

questions.explanation: Correct: CLAUDE.md is persistent project context auto-loaded each session and is a normal version-controlled file shared on clone/pull — it is not for secrets; reference credentials by name and supply them via env vars or a secret manager, moving any extra justification out of the file itself. A is wrong: there is no special encryption that makes committing secrets safe; a private repo still distributes the file to everyone with access. D is wrong: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded, not gated behind a slash command, so the secret was loaded and shared. B is wrong: CLAUDE.md has no 'settings.local.json frontmatter,' and that is not a secret-hiding mechanism.

questions.q: A team wants Claude Code to query their internal observability platform (logs, traces) and their issue tracker directly while debugging. They want these external tools exposed to Claude in a standard way that several teammates can share. What is the appropriate configuration?

options.A: Add both integrations as `~/.claude/commands/` entries so each developer manually pulls the logs and issues whenever they need them
options.B: Configure MCP servers for the observability platform and issue tracker so Claude calls those tools through the Model Context Protocol
options.C: Paste representative API responses from both systems into CLAUDE.md so the relevant data is always present in the session context
options.D: Write a PostToolUse hook that scrapes the observability dashboards and the issue tracker's web UI automatically after every single file edit in the session completes

questions.explanation: Correct: MCP (Model Context Protocol) server configuration is the standard way to expose external tools/data sources (like observability and issue trackers) to Claude. C is wrong: CLAUDE.md is static auto-loaded context, not a live data feed, and pasting responses gets stale immediately. D is wrong: a PostToolUse hook runs deterministic automation after tools; it is not a way to provide queryable external tooling. A is wrong: personal slash commands are manual shortcuts, not a shared, standardized external-tool integration.

questions.q: A support agent hits only 55% first-contact resolution (target 80%). Logs show it escalates easy cases (standard photo-evidence damage replacements) while trying to autonomously handle complex policy-exception cases. What is the most effective first step to fix escalation calibration?

options.A: Add sentiment analysis to the pipeline and escalate any case where the customer's detected frustration level crosses a high mark.
options.B: Train a separate classifier on historical resolved tickets to predict, before the agent runs, whether each incoming case should escalate.
options.C: Have the agent self-report a numeric 1-10 confidence score on each case and automatically escalate it whenever that reported score falls below a preset threshold.
options.D: Add explicit, decomposed escalation criteria to the system prompt with few-shot examples contrasting 'escalate' versus 'resolve autonomously.'

questions.explanation: Unclear decision boundaries are fixed first with explicit decomposed criteria plus contrasting few-shot examples. D is correct. C relies on poorly-calibrated self-reported confidence — the agent is already wrongly confident on hard cases. B jumps to ML infrastructure prematurely. A conflates customer sentiment with case complexity, which is the wrong signal for escalation.

questions.q: A document-QA system places a 30-page policy manual in the context, then a long set of formatting and citation rules at the very top, then the user's question last. Reviewers find the model frequently ignores the citation rules buried above the manual. Which fix best addresses the likely cause?

options.A: Remove all of the few-shot examples from the prompt so that the overall context is shorter and the rules are easier to reach
options.B: Restate the critical citation rules right before the user's question and near any examples, where attention is strongest
options.C: Set the decoding temperature to exactly 0 to force the model to deterministically obey the citation rules that currently sit at the very top of the long manual context
options.D: Delete the system prompt entirely so that nothing at the top of the context competes with the policy manual for the model's attention

questions.explanation: Correct (B): this is attention dilution / 'lost in the middle' — instructions far from the end of a long context get less weight, so the fix is to move or restate the critical rules adjacent to the examples and just before the user message. D throws away necessary task framing and does not change where the key rules sit relative to the content. C is the temperature misconception; lowering it does not relocate attention or fix instruction-following over long context. A removes useful guidance and still leaves the rules stranded far from the question.

questions.q: A loan-approval pipeline must run three steps in a fixed order: fraud_check() → credit_pull() → record_decision(). The orchestrator prompt says 'Always call fraud_check before credit_pull, and never record a decision without both.' Audit logs show that in 0.4% of runs the model called credit_pull() first, and in 0.1% it recorded a decision after only fraud_check(). Compliance requires zero out-of-order executions. What is the correct fix?

options.A: Lower the sampling temperature to 0 so the model deterministically and repeatably follows the ordering instruction on every request
options.B: Add an evaluator subagent that reviews each completed run, flags out-of-order executions, and routes the affected loans for manual reprocessing
options.C: Enforce the ordering in code with a deterministic state machine that tracks which steps have completed, exposes only the single valid next tool at each stage, and structurally refuses any out-of-sequence call by making the invalid tools unavailable until their prerequisites are satisfied
options.D: Strengthen the system prompt with capitalized ordering warnings and a few-shot example

questions.explanation: Compliance requires zero violations on a critical fixed sequence, which only deterministic/programmatic enforcement can guarantee. C is correct: a state machine or guarded wrapper makes out-of-order calls structurally impossible regardless of what the model chooses, because only the valid next tool is ever exposed and any other call is refused. D relies on probabilistic prompt compliance — the very thing already failing at 0.4%/0.1%; stronger wording and examples reduce but never eliminate the rate. B is reactive: it detects violations only after they happen and the decision was already recorded, and it adds manual toil rather than preventing the error. A is a misconception: temperature 0 makes sampling greedy/more repeatable but does not make instruction-following guaranteed, and the model can still deterministically choose a wrong order on some inputs.

questions.q: A user asks an agent, 'What's the current value of the TIMEOUT_MS constant in config/app.yaml?' The team's framework is configured to spin up a coordinator that decomposes every request into planning, research, and synthesis subagents. For this request the overhead adds ~30s and three extra model calls. What is the right architectural judgment?

options.A: Spawn one subagent per config file in the repository to thoroughly search every location where the requested constant might possibly be defined
options.B: Add a fourth caching subagent that stores resolved constant values so repeated lookups of the same config key return faster next time
options.C: Keep the planning, research, and synthesis decomposition so that every request follows the same uniform, predictable processing pipeline
options.D: Handle it directly by reading the one named file and returning the value; a single-fact lookup does not justify multi-agent orchestration

questions.explanation: Multi-agent orchestration earns its overhead only when a task has genuine breadth, parallelism, or context-isolation needs. D is correct: a single known file and a single fact is trivially handled inline; the orchestration is pure overhead. C values uniformity over fitness — forcing every trivial query through a heavy pipeline wastes latency and tokens. B invents complexity (a caching agent) to speed up a process that should not exist for this task. A is over-decomposition: fanning out across all config files to find a value the user already located in one named file multiplies cost for no benefit.

questions.q: A coordinator must produce a competitive analysis that requires (1) scraping three competitor sites, (2) summarizing each, and (3) a final cross-competitor synthesis. The three scrape+summarize chains are independent; only the synthesis needs all three. The current implementation runs everything strictly one item at a time. Latency is 3x higher than necessary. What is the best restructuring?

options.A: Run the three scrape+summarize chains concurrently, then synthesize once all three return — one barrier before synthesis only.
options.B: Launch all four steps, synthesis included, concurrently from the very start and reconcile any ordering issues after they all finish.
options.C: Keep the chains sequential but simply drop the two slowest competitors so only one scrape+summarize runs and total latency falls.
options.D: Merge the three competitors into one large prompt and have a single agent scrape, summarize, and synthesize them in one pass.

questions.explanation: Independent work should run in parallel with a barrier only before the dependent step. A is correct. C sacrifices coverage by dropping competitors. B is incorrect because synthesis genuinely depends on the three results and cannot start before they exist. D discards the parallelism and overloads a single context with all the raw scraping.

questions.q: A long compliance prompt is organized as one continuous wall of text mixing the role, the source documents, the rules, and the user's request, and the model frequently treats the source documents as instructions (or vice versa). Without changing the model or temperature, what is the most effective structural change?

options.A: Delete the source documents from the prompt entirely so that there is no longer any document text left for the model to confuse with the instructions
options.B: Wrap each part in clear delimiters (XML-style tags like <role>, <documents>, <rules>, <question>) to separate instructions from data, with the rules near the question
options.C: Set the decoding temperature to exactly 0 so that generation is fully deterministic and the model reliably stops confusing the embedded source documents with the actual compliance instructions it must follow
options.D: Merge every section together into a single uninterrupted paragraph so that the model reads the whole prompt as one coherent, continuous instruction block

questions.explanation: Correct (B): XML/section delimiters give the model explicit structure to distinguish instructions from data, and putting the rules near the question counters attention dilution — both are prompt-structure fixes that don't touch the model or temperature. D removes the very boundaries that prevent confusion. C is the temperature misconception; instruction/data confusion is a structure problem, not a randomness problem. A destroys the inputs the task needs, 'solving' the confusion by removing the work.

questions.q: A single Claude Code session has run for hours on a large refactor. As the conversation approaches the context-window limit, what does the harness do to let work continue without losing the thread — and what should you (the agent) assume about it?

options.A: It automatically raises the underlying model's physical token limit on the fly, so the available context is effectively unlimited and needs no active management at all.
options.B: It terminates the session at the limit and forces the user to restart from scratch, so long tasks should never be run in one session.
