options.B: Add longer timeouts and a health check before each step so the workflow is far less likely to crash mid-way and therefore rarely needs a retry at all
options.C: Make each step idempotent using a stable idempotency key (e.g., the order id) and checkpoint completed steps, so a retry skips already-applied effects and resumes from the failure point
options.D: Wrap the four steps in a try/catch that, on any failure, rolls everything back and re-runs the entire workflow cleanly from the beginning on the next attempt

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is retry safety over side effects: idempotency keys plus checkpointing make re-execution provably safe — a replayed charge or reservation is recognized and skipped — while preserving automatic recovery. The strong runner-up is D (rollback then full re-run): compensating transactions are a legitimate pattern, but a full rollback-and-restart still re-executes side effects on retry and assumes every external effect (a sent email, a captured charge) is cleanly reversible, which often it is not. A loses because it sacrifices the automatic recovery the team explicitly wants to keep. B loses because reducing crash frequency does not make the retries that still happen safe — it lowers the odds of the bug without fixing it.

questions.q: A team is deciding whether to build a multi-agent orchestration for a task that classifies an incoming support email into one of five categories and drafts a one-line acknowledgment. Volume is high and the task is well-bounded. What is the BEST architectural recommendation?

options.A: Use an orchestrator with one subagent per category so each specialist can be tuned for the language patterns of the class it owns over time
options.B: Adopt an evaluator-optimizer loop where a drafting agent proposes the acknowledgment and a critic agent iterates until phrasing quality converges
options.C: Use a multi-agent design with a classifier subagent and a drafting subagent so each component stays focused and can be evaluated independently
options.D: Handle it with a single agent (or a single model call), since the task is trivial and orchestration overhead would add cost and latency for no benefit

questions.explanation: The decisive facts are that the task is trivial and well-bounded at high volume, where multi-agent overhead (extra calls, coordination latency, cost) buys nothing. D is correct. C and A add structure that a single call handles, and A's per-category fan-out is especially wasteful. B applies an evaluator-optimizer pattern, which is appropriate only when output quality is hard to get right in one shot and you have actionable criteria plus bounded iteration; for a one-line acknowledgment that is over-engineering. C and B are defensible-sounding precisely because those patterns are good elsewhere, but not for this trivial workload.

questions.q: A binary spam/not-spam classifier runs at very high QPS under a tight per-request latency budget, and on a labeled set it is already highly accurate emitting a one-token label directly. An engineer proposes adding a chain-of-thought step "to be safe," having the model reason through each message before answering. Given the constraints, what is the BEST recommendation?

options.A: Add a brief chain-of-thought before the label so the model reasons through every message, accepting the extra tokens as a safety margin on accuracy
options.B: Do not add chain-of-thought; keep the direct one-token classification, optionally reinforcing it with a few-shot examples or a constrained enum label
options.C: Raise the temperature slightly so the model explores more before committing to a label, improving robustness on the genuinely ambiguous messages
options.D: Add chain-of-thought reasoning but cap it at a short fixed token length so that the reasoning cost stays bounded per request while still providing some accuracy cushion on the harder, more ambiguous messages

questions.explanation: B wins because the task is simple and already highly accurate, so CoT adds latency and token cost with no accuracy benefit — CoT is not universally helpful — and the latency-sensitive, high-QPS setting makes the direct label (optionally a few-shot or enum) the right call. A is the tempting runner-up: "reasoning is safer" sounds prudent, but here it spends the scarce latency budget for no measured gain, which is the decisive constraint. D is the same mistake with a smaller bill — still added cost for no demonstrated accuracy lift. C injects randomness into a classification that benefits from determinism, risking accuracy rather than improving it.

questions.q: Your team needs to share MCP server configuration (an internal database tool and an API connector) so that every developer who clones the repo automatically gets the same servers, version-controlled with the code. Which configuration location is the intended mechanism?

options.A: Commit a .mcp.json file at the project root; it is the project-scoped MCP config that is checked into the repo and picked up automatically by everyone who clones it.
options.B: Store the servers only in .claude/settings.local.json so the configuration travels with the project checkout while remaining git-ignored.
options.C: Register the servers interactively with `claude mcp add` on each machine, which is the only supported way to make MCP servers available in a project.
options.D: Put the server definitions in each developer's ~/.claude.json user config so the team converges on the same setup over time as people copy it.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A. A committed .mcp.json at the project root is the purpose-built, version-controlled way to share MCP servers with everyone who clones the repository. Runner-up is B: settings.local.json does travel with the checkout, but it is git-ignored and personal, so it is never shared with teammates—the opposite of the goal. D relies on each user's global config and only converges by manual copying, defeating uniform enforcement. C describes a real command, but it is per-machine and not version-controlled, and it is not the only mechanism—committed .mcp.json is.

questions.q: An IT help desk receives wildly different request types — password resets, VPN troubleshooting, software-license purchasing approvals, and HR policy questions — each needing different tools, knowledge, and tone. A single all-purpose prompt handles the mix but frequently grabs the wrong tool and mixes policies across domains. Which design most directly addresses this?

options.A: Raise the model size and lower temperature so the single prompt reasons more carefully about which tool and policy applies to each request
options.B: A router that first classifies the incoming request type, then dispatches it to a specialized handler scoped to only that domain's tools, knowledge, and tone
options.C: Keep one prompt but expand it with detailed per-category instructions and the full tool catalog so the model has everything it needs to handle any request in context
options.D: Add a verification subagent after the single prompt that checks whether the response used the right tools and policies, looping back for correction when it detects a mismatch

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is that the request types are genuinely heterogeneous with disjoint tools and knowledge, so classify-then-dispatch isolates each handler and eliminates cross-domain bleed at the source. The strong runner-up is C (one richer prompt with all tools): consolidating works for homogeneous traffic, but loading every domain's tools and policies into one context is precisely what causes the wrong-tool grabs and policy mixing here — more context makes the confusion worse, not better. D loses because a post-hoc verifier patches symptoms after the fact and adds a correction loop, rather than preventing the misroute. A loses because the failure is architectural overload, not a reasoning-horsepower or temperature deficit, so a bigger model still juggles incompatible domains.

questions.q: A consulting firm needs to produce a due-diligence report on an acquisition target: simultaneously analyzing the target's financial filings, scraping and summarizing competitor press coverage, reviewing its open-source code repositories for license risk, and assessing its patent portfolio. Each strand is large, open-ended, and can proceed independently, and the firm wants the lead to synthesize them at the end. Which architecture fits best?

options.A: A fixed deterministic workflow that always runs the same four scripted steps in the same order, since the deliverable structure is known in advance and predictability matters
options.B: A prompt chain where finance output feeds press analysis, which feeds the code review, which feeds the patent assessment, so each stage builds on the last
options.C: An orchestrator that spawns four specialized subagents — one per strand — each with its own scoped brief and tools, running in parallel and returning distilled findings the lead merges into the report
options.D: A single agent with one long system prompt that walks through finance, then press, then code, then patents sequentially within a single conversation so that all gathered context stays co-located and available for the final synthesis step

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is genuine breadth: four large, independent investigation strands that each justify their own context window and tooling, with a synthesis step at the end — the canonical orchestrator-worker case where parallel subagents pay off. The strong runner-up is B (prompt chaining): chaining is excellent when stages truly depend on each other, but here the strands are independent, so forcing a chain serializes parallelizable work and pollutes each stage with irrelevant upstream context for no gain. D loses because cramming four deep investigations into one context exhausts the window and degrades reasoning — the opposite of distillation. A loses because the work is open-ended and exploratory (judgment about license and patent risk), not a fixed scripted pipeline, so a rigid workflow can't adapt to what each strand uncovers.

questions.q: A support assistant writes multi-paragraph answers grounded in a set of provided knowledge-base articles. The articles are accurate, but the long answers sometimes assert specifics — version numbers, limits, dates — that appear nowhere in the sources. Leadership needs every factual claim to be traceable to the supplied documents. What is the MOST effective change?

options.A: Add a closing instruction telling the model to be accurate and to avoid making things up when it writes the multi-paragraph support answer
options.B: Move the knowledge-base articles to the very top of the prompt so they sit far from the question and benefit from primacy positioning
options.C: Lower the temperature toward 0 so the model becomes more conservative and is far less likely to invent the unsupported version numbers, limits, and dates that currently appear in its long multi-paragraph answers
options.D: Instruct the model to base every claim only on the provided articles and to quote or cite the exact supporting passage for each factual statement it makes

questions.explanation: D wins because requiring each claim to be tied to a quoted/cited source passage forces grounding and makes unsupported specifics visible and avoidable — directly satisfying the traceability requirement. C is the strong runner-up — lower temperature does modestly reduce confabulation — but it never enforces grounding; a low-temperature model can still confidently state an unsupported version number. A is a vague exhortation with no mechanism behind it. B adjusts positioning but the articles are already available; placement is not why the model invents specifics, so it does not establish traceability.

questions.q: A team extracting structured data has already forced a tool call whose input is a strict JSON Schema. Most outputs validate, but about 1% fail schema validation (for example, an enum value slightly off). The integration cannot tolerate any invalid payload reaching the database under any circumstances. Which approach MOST reliably guarantees that only valid payloads are persisted?

options.A: Keep schema-validating the emitted tool output and, on any validation failure, programmatically retry the call before anything at all is written downstream
options.B: Increase the count of few-shot examples that cover each of the allowed enum values so that the stubborn 1% of edge cases gradually disappears
options.C: Prefill the assistant turn with the opening braces of the JSON object so that the overall structure is locked in firmly from the very first token
options.D: Drop the temperature parameter to 0 to eliminate the sampling variance that is held responsible for the occasional out-of-range bad enum value occasionally appearing

questions.explanation: A is correct: validate-then-retry at the tool layer is the reliability mechanism that guarantees only conforming payloads pass, because invalid ones are caught and never persisted. B may reduce the rate but cannot guarantee zero invalid payloads reach the DB. C (prefilling) shapes the start of the output but still requires validation and does not enforce enum correctness mid-structure. D reduces randomness but does not enforce the schema; a deterministic run can still emit an off-enum value, and it provides no gate before the write.

questions.q: A production service calling the API intermittently receives 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses during traffic spikes. The current client catches these and immediately re-issues the same request in a tight loop until it succeeds. Failures get worse during spikes. What is the correct reliability fix?

options.A: Switch every request to the Message Batches API during spikes, because batched jobs are exempt from rate limits and overload conditions on the synchronous endpoint
options.B: Keep retrying immediately in the tight loop but split each call across two API keys, so the doubled request rate clears the backlog faster during the spike
options.C: Stop retrying entirely and fail fast on the first 429 or 529, since these status codes indicate the request itself is malformed and will never succeed
options.D: Retry the failed requests, but space attempts using exponential backoff with random jitter, capping the number of retries before surfacing the error to the caller

questions.explanation: 429/529 are transient capacity signals. Exponential backoff with jitter spreads retries out so the server can recover and avoids synchronized retry storms, with a retry cap so failures eventually surface. The tight loop is itself worsening the spike by hammering an already-strained endpoint. C is the strong-ish wrong answer but loses on a factual error: 429/529 are transient, not malformed-request errors (that would be 4xx like 400), so failing fast needlessly drops recoverable requests. B makes it worse — adding more immediate load via a second key increases the pressure that caused the throttling. A is false: batch jobs are not magically exempt from capacity limits and introduce up-to-24h latency unacceptable for a synchronous service.

questions.q: A multi-step research agent passes its full accumulated transcript, including large raw tool dumps from earlier steps, into every subsequent subtask. Later subtasks have started producing answers that reference data unrelated to the current question. Which explanation and remedy is MOST accurate?

options.A: The earlier tool dumps are polluting later subtasks; isolate irrelevant data so each subtask sees only what it needs
options.B: Prompt caching replayed a stale earlier response, so caching should be turned off to stop the cross-contamination
options.C: The transcript exceeded the window and was compacted, so the agent is hallucinating from a lossy summary it should disable
options.D: The tool dumps were re-read and cached permanently, so the agent must clear the permanent cache between subtasks

questions.explanation: Carrying irrelevant earlier data into later subtasks is classic context pollution; the remedy is to isolate each subtask so it only receives relevant context. C misattributes the issue to compaction, which carries a faithful summary rather than inventing unrelated data, and disabling it would not help. B is wrong because caching reuses prompt prefixes, not final responses, so it cannot replay a stale answer. D is wrong on two counts: there is no permanent cache, and re-reading re-injects tokens rather than caching them forever.

questions.q: One prompt asks the model, in a single pass, to (1) translate a customer email, (2) classify its intent, (3) extract order IDs, (4) draft a reply, and (5) flag compliance risks. Output quality is poor on every one of the five tasks at once. The instructions for each task are individually clear and correct. What is the MOST effective restructuring?

options.A: Set temperature to 0 across the combined prompt so all five tasks are handled deterministically and stop varying between runs
options.B: Keep the single prompt but add several more clarifying sentences and constraints under each of the five task headings to tighten the instructions
options.C: Decompose the work into a chain of focused steps, each prompt (or call) handling one subtask and passing its output to the next
options.D: Reorder the five tasks so the most important one appears last, using recency to make sure at least that task is handled well

questions.explanation: C wins because cramming five distinct objectives into one pass overloads the model and degrades all of them; splitting into a chain of focused steps lets each call do one thing well. B is the strong runner-up — clearer per-task instructions is normally sound — but the instructions are already clear; the bottleneck is doing five things simultaneously, so more text in the same overloaded prompt will not fix it. D only rescues one task at the expense of the rest. A reduces randomness but a deterministically mediocre five-in-one answer is still mediocre.

questions.q: An orchestrator agent runs a payments workflow where it must (1) verify funds, (2) place a hold, then (3) capture the charge, strictly in that order. The system prompt clearly instructs the model to follow the sequence. In load testing, roughly 1 in 400 runs captures before the hold is confirmed, double-charging the customer. What is the MOST effective fix?

options.A: Lower the sampling temperature for the orchestrator so the model adheres more reliably to the documented step order across runs
options.B: Wrap the three steps in code so capture is structurally unreachable until the hold call returns a confirmed status, removing ordering from the model's discretion
options.C: Move the ordering rules into a dedicated, emphasized section of the system prompt and add few-shot examples that demonstrate the correct sequence under edge cases
options.D: Add a validation subagent that reviews the orchestrator's planned tool calls and flags any out-of-order sequence before execution proceeds

questions.explanation: The decisive constraint is irreversible financial harm at a residual error rate that prompt-based methods cannot drive to zero. B enforces the sequence deterministically in code, so the unsafe path is structurally impossible. C and A only improve probabilistic compliance and leave a non-zero failure rate, unacceptable for double-charging. D is the strong runner-up and genuinely helpful, but it is still a model reviewing a model (probabilistic) and adds latency; a critical, well-defined sequence belongs in deterministic control flow, not in another LLM check.

questions.q: An evaluator-optimizer loop improves generated SQL: a generator writes a query, an evaluator scores it, and the generator revises. In production the loop sometimes runs 30+ iterations, occasionally never terminating, and the evaluator's feedback is often just a numeric score with no detail. Which pair of changes most directly fixes the loop's design flaws?

options.A: Add a hard maximum iteration count with a fallback, and have the evaluator return specific, actionable critique the generator can act on each round
options.B: Run the generator and evaluator in parallel so feedback arrives sooner, and log every iteration to a store for later offline analysis of failures
options.C: Cache previously generated queries so the generator can reuse close matches, and lower the evaluator's threshold to make convergence faster
options.D: Replace the single evaluator with three evaluators that vote, and raise the passing score threshold so only high-quality queries are accepted

questions.explanation: Evaluator-optimizer loops require two things to be sound: bounded iteration (so they always terminate) and actionable criteria (so each revision is informed rather than a blind retry). A delivers exactly both, fixing the runaway-loop and the uninformative-score problems. D adds evaluators and raises the bar, which can worsen non-termination, not fix it, and does nothing about feedback quality. C trades correctness for speed and still has no iteration bound. B is incoherent: the evaluator depends on the generator's output, so they cannot run in parallel, and logging helps debugging but not the live loop.

questions.q: A scheduling tool `set_status(ticket_id, status)` takes `status` as a free-form string. The valid statuses are exactly open, pending, resolved, and closed, but the model keeps passing values like 'in progress', 'Done', or 'resolved.' that the backend rejects, causing failed calls and retries. The set of valid values is small and fixed. What is the most reliable fix?

options.A: Return a structured error listing the four valid values whenever an invalid status is submitted, so the model can correct itself on the next attempt
options.B: Keep accepting any string and add a server-side fuzzy-matching layer that maps near-miss inputs like 'Done' onto the closest legal status before applying it
options.C: Constrain the `status` parameter to an enum of the four valid values in the input schema so invalid statuses cannot be passed in the first place
options.D: Keep `status` as a free string but document the four allowed values in the tool description and add an example call showing the correct spelling of each

questions.explanation: The decisive fact is a small, fixed set of valid values, which is the textbook case for an enum constraint in the input schema — the model is shown the allowed set and invalid values become structurally impossible to send (C). The strong runner-up is A (structured error listing the valid values) — good error design that does help the model self-correct, but it loses because it only fixes mistakes after a failed call and round-trip, whereas the enum prevents the bad call entirely; given the value set is fixed, prevention beats reactive correction. D relies on the model honoring prose, which it already is not doing. B silently guesses intent and can map to the wrong status (e.g. 'Done' to 'resolved' vs 'closed'), introducing data corruption.

questions.q: An agent enriches 5,000 leads by calling a third-party enrichment API that allows 10 concurrent requests and returns 429s beyond that. The current implementation fires all 5,000 calls at once; the API rejects most, the agent's blanket retry storms it further, and the whole job collapses. The team still needs the work parallelized to finish on time. What is the best fix?

options.A: Keep firing all 5,000 at once but add exponential backoff with jitter to every retry so the storm eventually spreads out and the calls succeed
options.B: Bound concurrency with a semaphore/worker pool capped at the API's 10-request limit, so at most 10 calls are in flight at any time while the rest queue
options.C: Split the 5,000 leads across more parallel agent instances so each handles a smaller batch and the total load is distributed across workers
options.D: Process the 5,000 leads strictly one at a time in a single sequential pass, eliminating concurrency entirely so the third-party API is never overwhelmed by simultaneous requests and never returns a 429 under any circumstances

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is a known downstream concurrency ceiling: a semaphore/pool capped at 10 keeps the pipeline parallel and fast while never exceeding what the API tolerates — matching offered load to capacity. The strong runner-up is A (backoff with jitter): retry backoff is correct and necessary practice, but it only reacts after the API is already saturated by 5,000 simultaneous calls; without an in-flight cap the system keeps overshooting and thrashing. D loses because fully serializing throws away the parallelism the team needs to finish on time — over-correcting. C loses, and is worse, because more agent instances raise total concurrency against the same shared 10-request limit, intensifying the 429 storm rather than bounding it.

questions.q: A pipeline has steps: A (fetch user profile), B (fetch product catalog), C (fetch inventory), and D (generate a personalized recommendation that needs the outputs of A, B, and C). A, B, and C are independent network calls of similar latency. The team wants to minimize end-to-end latency. Which orchestration is correct?

options.A: Run A, then B, then C sequentially to keep error handling simple, and run D once the third call returns its result to the orchestrator
options.B: Run A and B in parallel, then run C, and finally run D, overlapping only the two calls that share an upstream dependency
options.C: Run A, B, C, and D all in parallel and have D retry internally until the other three calls have populated their shared results
options.D: Run A, B, and C in parallel, then place a barrier so D begins only after all three have returned successfully

questions.explanation: A, D, C are independent, so they should run concurrently; B depends on all three, so a single barrier before B is exactly right. D states this. A serializes independent work and inflates latency for no benefit. C is wrong because B cannot start before its inputs exist; retry-spinning wastes calls and risks acting on partial data. B invents a dependency between A and C that the scenario does not state and only partially parallelizes, leaving latency on the table. The lever here is the latency budget plus true independence.

questions.q: An API returns a JSON object describing a person. When a middle name is absent the model sometimes emits "middle_name": "N/A", sometimes an empty string, sometimes "none", and sometimes drops the key entirely, breaking strict downstream parsing. The other fields are fine. Which prompt change MOST directly stops the inconsistent absence formats?

options.A: Add a single few-shot example that shows a fully complete person record with all three of the name fields present and populated with real values
options.B: Specify each field's type and state that an absent middle name MUST be the JSON literal null, with the key always present in the object
options.C: Append the sentence "Be consistent about how you represent any missing values" to the very end of the existing instruction block
options.D: Lower the temperature parameter to 0 so that the model picks the very same absence representation on every request it handles

questions.explanation: B is correct: hallucinated absence formats are stopped by specifying field types and giving an explicit absence contract (always-present key, value null). A shows only a populated case, teaching nothing about the absent case. C is a vague instruction that names the goal without defining the one acceptable representation, so the model still has to guess. D reduces variance but the model may still deterministically pick a wrong representation like "N/B"; determinism is not the same as the required null contract.

questions.q: A workspace exposes a genuinely useful tool `reconcile_ledger(period)`, but telemetry shows the model almost never selects it even in situations where it is exactly the right call. Its current description reads 'Ledger utility for accounting tasks.' The tool's schema and behavior are correct; only its selection rate is poor. What change will most improve how often the model picks it appropriately?

options.A: Add two optional parameters (`currency`, `granularity`) so the tool can handle more variations of accounting requests and therefore matches more queries
options.B: Lower the temperature of the model so its tool-selection sampling becomes more deterministic and it stops skipping the relevant tool
options.C: Rewrite the description to state precisely what the tool does and the exact conditions under which the model should call it versus alternatives
options.D: Rename the tool from `reconcile_ledger` to `accounting_ledger_reconciliation_tool_v2` so its purpose is clearer from the identifier the model sees

questions.explanation: The decisive fact is that the tool is correct but under-selected, and the description is vague. The description is the primary signal the model uses to choose a tool, so making it precise about what it does and when to use it directly fixes selection (C). The strong runner-up is D (renaming) — a clearer name does help selection somewhat and is good practice, but the name is a far weaker signal than the description and a verbose name cannot convey when to use it versus alternatives; the vague description remains the bottleneck. A adds capability nobody said was missing and does nothing for selection. B changes sampling determinism but cannot make the model favor a tool it has no clear reason to pick.

questions.q: A document-processing system uses one orchestrator and several subagents. Latency is dominated by the orchestrator waiting on a single subagent that summarizes a 200-page contract, while three other independent subagents finish quickly and sit idle. A dependent finalization step needs all four outputs. Which is the MOST effective NEXT step to cut end-to-end latency?

options.A: Switch the orchestrator to a faster, smaller model so it issues and collects the four subagent calls with less per-step overhead during the run
options.B: Remove the barrier and let the finalization step begin as soon as the three fast subagents return, incorporating the contract summary whenever it arrives
options.C: Run the three fast subagents sequentially after the slow one so the orchestrator manages fewer concurrent calls and reduces coordination complexity overall
options.D: Split the long contract into sections summarized by parallel sub-subagents, then combine, so the slow path shrinks before the barrier the finalize step needs

questions.explanation: The critical path is the single long summarization; the fast subagents already run concurrently, so the only way to cut end-to-end latency is to shorten that longest task. D parallelizes the slow work itself (map-reduce over sections), directly attacking the bottleneck before the necessary barrier. C serializes independent work and makes things worse. B is tempting but unsound: the finalize step genuinely depends on all four outputs, so removing the barrier risks acting on incomplete data. A targets orchestrator overhead, which is negligible compared to the 200-page summarization dominating latency.

questions.q: Your team wants a convention that all .env files are added to .gitignore as a baseline safety habit across every project you personally work on—without forcing teammates to adopt it. Where should this convention live: your ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the project's .claude/CLAUDE.md, or neither?

options.A: In ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, since user-level memory applies to you across all projects without imposing the convention on teammates.
options.B: In a shared skill at .claude/skills/env-safety/SKILL.md, so any teammate can invoke the safety check explicitly whenever they need it.
options.C: In ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, because that file is automatically synced into every teammate's environment the moment they clone the shared repository and start a session.
options.D: In the project's .claude/CLAUDE.md committed to the repo, since that guarantees the safety rule is applied uniformly for every team member on the project.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A. CLAUDE.md scope is the lever: project .claude/CLAUDE.md is committed and applies to the whole team, while ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is user-level and follows only you across all your projects. For a personal habit you want everywhere but must not force on others, the user-level file is the right home. Runner-up is C, which also names ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md but is wrong about its mechanics: the user-level file lives on your machine and is never inherited by teammates who clone the repo. D is factually fine but defeats the goal by forcing the rule on everyone. B is wrong because skills package invokable capabilities, not always-applied conventions like a .gitignore habit.

questions.q: A deployment tool `apply_migration(plan_id)` fails intermittently and currently surfaces failures by returning the raw runtime stack trace as a 500-style opaque text string. The agent reading this cannot tell whether to retry, fix its input, or stop — so it loops blindly. You want the agent to recover autonomously when recovery is possible. What is the best redesign of the failure path?

options.A: Return the full stack trace but prepend a short human-readable sentence summarizing the exception so the agent has a friendlier message to read at the top
options.B: Return a structured error object containing an error-type code, a boolean retryable flag, and the offending field so the agent can branch on how to respond
options.C: Suppress errors entirely and return a generic success acknowledgement, logging the real failure to an internal dashboard the operations team monitors separately
options.D: Keep the opaque string but attach the migration's correlation id so an engineer can later look up the full context of the failure in the logs

questions.explanation: The decisive constraint is that the agent must decide autonomously whether to retry, fix input, or stop — which requires machine-readable signals. A structured error with type, retryable flag, and offending field gives the agent exactly those branch points (B). The strong runner-up is A (stack trace plus a summary sentence) — adding a readable summary is better than nothing and looks helpful, but free text is still not reliably parseable; the agent cannot deterministically branch on prose, so it still risks blind looping. C hides the failure and produces false success — the worst outcome for an agent. D aids human post-mortems but gives the live agent no actionable signal to recover.

questions.q: A summarization prompt must output a JSON object whose "sentiment" field is constrained to exactly "positive", "negative", or "neutral". The model usually complies but occasionally returns "mixed" or "slightly positive", which downstream code does not expect. The team wants the strongest possible guarantee that only the three allowed values ever appear. Which option is the LEAST effective at providing that guarantee?

options.A: Constrain the field with a JSON Schema enum holding exactly the three permitted values, enforced at the tool layer on every emitted call
options.B: Define the field as a tool-input enum restricted to the three values and validate the emitted tool call, retrying on any out-of-enum result
options.C: Tell the model in the prompt to choose one of exactly three values and to reject anything else, relying on it to faithfully self-enforce the stated rule
options.D: Validate each returned sentiment value against a fixed allow-list of the three permitted strings and reject-and-retry whenever the returned value misses that list

questions.explanation: C is the LEAST effective (the EXCEPT-style answer): a prompt-level instruction asks the model to self-enforce the enum, which is precisely what is already failing — instructions cannot hard-guarantee allowed values. B, D, and A all enforce the constraint outside the model: B and A use a schema/enum validated at the tool layer with retry, and D uses an explicit allow-list check with reject-and-retry. Each of those provides an actual gate that blocks disallowed values, whereas C leaves the guarantee to the model's compliance, which is unreliable.

questions.q: Your project needs Claude to be able to query your internal ticketing system and your observability platform during sessions, exposing those as callable tools. All of the following are appropriate building blocks EXCEPT:

options.A: Configuring MCP servers that expose the ticketing and observability systems as tools Claude can call
options.B: Defining a project command in .claude/commands/ that wraps a common multi-step ticket triage workflow
options.C: Using settings.local.json to keep your own personal MCP server configuration uncommitted to the repo
options.D: Storing the API tokens for those systems as plaintext values inside the project CLAUDE.md for Claude

questions.explanation: The EXCEPT answer is D: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded context and must never hold secrets like API tokens, since it is committed and injected into context. The other three are all legitimate: MCP servers (A) are exactly how external systems are exposed as tools; settings.local.json (C) is the right place for personal, uncommitted config including MCP setup; and a project command (B) is a fine way to package a repeatable triage workflow. The trap is that D is phrased as a convenient way to 'let Claude read the tokens,' which is precisely the anti-pattern.

questions.q: Your team requires that every time Claude edits a Python file, `black` runs on that file with no exceptions, on every developer's machine, enforced by the harness rather than relying on the model to remember. Which configuration is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Define a PostToolUse hook in the committed .claude/settings.json that runs black on the edited file
options.B: Build a skill that packages a black-formatting capability and tell the team to invoke it after edits
options.C: Create a .claude/rules/ file with a *.py glob frontmatter describing the black formatting convention
options.D: Add an instruction to the project CLAUDE.md saying to always run black after editing a Python file

questions.explanation: The decisive constraint is deterministic, zero-exception enforcement run by the harness. Only PostToolUse hooks in settings.json are executed mechanically by the harness after every matching tool call, independent of the model's judgment. CLAUDE.md (D) is auto-loaded context the model may follow inconsistently, not a guarantee. Rules (C) inject path-based guidance into context but still depend on the model acting on them. D skill (B) is a reusable capability that must be invoked, not an automatic always-run trigger. D and C are the kind of guidance that 'usually works' but is not deterministic, making them the strong-looking but wrong choices.

questions.q: A security policy mandates that nobody can run `rm -rf` against the production backup directory /backup, and it must be stopped before execution rather than detected after the fact. Which combination of hook timing and permission rule gives the strongest guarantee?

options.A: Add only the deny rule Bash(rm -rf /backup); since deny rules are evaluated first in permission precedence, a hook would be redundant and add no protection at all.
options.B: Add a PreToolUse hook that denies the rm -rf /backup call before execution, plus a deny rule Bash(rm -rf /backup) so a denied permission also blocks it for defense in depth.
options.C: Add a PreToolUse hook that denies the command and deliberately omit any deny rule, so the hook remains the single, unambiguous point of enforcement for this policy.
options.D: Add a PostToolUse hook that inspects each Bash command and rejects rm -rf /backup right after it runs, backed by a deny rule for the same command pattern.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. PreToolUse fires before execution and is the only hook that can deny a call up front, and pairing it with a deny permission rule (deny is evaluated ahead of ask/allow) gives two independent layers, so the command is blocked even if one layer is misconfigured. Runner-up is A, which is right that a deny rule alone would block the command, but it dismisses defense in depth—two enforcement points are strictly safer than one for a destructive, irreversible action. D is wrong because PostToolUse fires after execution and cannot prevent it. C weakens the guarantee by relying on a single point of failure.

questions.q: A long-running autonomous agent makes an early architectural decision ('use the Postgres connection pool, not direct connections') and keeps working for hours. As context fills, older turns get compacted/summarized, and the agent later risks reintroducing direct connections because the decision is no longer reliably present in its window. What is the most robust way to preserve that decision?

options.A: Raise the agent's temperature toward 0 so its outputs become more deterministic and it stops drifting away from the decision it made earlier in the run
options.B: Disable compaction for this agent so the full transcript is always retained verbatim, guaranteeing the early decision is never dropped from the window
options.C: Write the decision and its rationale to an external durable store (a file or database) that the agent re-reads at each major step, instead of relying on it surviving compaction in context
options.D: Repeat the decision verbatim in every single tool result the agent receives, so the most recent context always restates it no matter how far the run has progressed

questions.explanation: Compaction is lossy by design, so anything that must survive indefinitely should live in durable external state the agent re-reads, not be trusted to remain in-context. B is the strong runner-up — never compacting would indeed keep the decision — but it loses on durability/scale: the context window is finite and cannot grow on the fly, so a long run will eventually exhaust it and force eviction or failure; you cannot simply turn compaction off forever. A confuses determinism with memory: temperature affects sampling, not whether a fact is still in the window. D is brittle and wasteful — it depends on the agent's own machinery to inject the text every time and still consumes window space, whereas an external store is the reliable source of truth.

questions.q: A support agent has two tools: a read-only `get_customer(id)` and a `delete_customer(id)` that permanently and irreversibly purges the account and all its data. Both are currently callable by the model fully autonomously with no gate. You must reduce catastrophic-mistake risk with minimal friction on normal work. What is the best policy?

options.A: Require human approval before every tool call from this agent, including reads, so that no action of any kind can happen without an operator explicitly signing off first
options.B: Add a strict input schema to `delete_customer` validating the id format, so malformed deletion requests are rejected before they can reach the backend
options.C: Leave both tools autonomous but add a nightly backup job so any account the agent deletes by mistake can be restored from the previous snapshot the next day
options.D: Put a human confirmation/approval gate in front of `delete_customer` because it is irreversible, while leaving the benign read-only `get_customer` ungated

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is irreversibility: the cost of a wrong `delete_customer` call cannot be undone, while a wrong read is cheap. Gating only the destructive tool targets risk where it matters and keeps friction off the benign read (D). The strong runner-up is A (gate everything) — maximally safe and defensible as a blanket policy, but it loses because the requirement is minimal friction on normal work; forcing approval on harmless reads adds cost with no risk reduction, so it over-applies the gate. C reduces but does not eliminate harm — a daily snapshot still loses up to a day of data and any post-snapshot changes, and irreversible means do not rely on undo. B blocks malformed ids but a perfectly well-formed id for the wrong customer still executes an irreversible purge.

questions.q: An interactive coding assistant produces long answers (often 800-1,200 tokens). Users complain it 'feels frozen' for several seconds before anything appears, even though total completion time is acceptable. The product goal is to make each reply feel responsive in the live chat UI. Which approach best addresses the complaint?

options.A: Stream the response token-by-token so the user sees output begin almost immediately, improving time-to-first-token and perceived responsiveness during the long generation
options.B: Enable prompt caching on the shared system prompt, on the assumption that a warm cached prefix is what actually governs how quickly the first visible token reaches the user in an interactive chat UI
options.C: Submit these chat turns through the Message Batches API to benefit from its discount and higher aggregate throughput across many concurrent users
options.D: Raise the model's maximum output tokens so the full answer is delivered in one larger chunk instead of arriving in smaller, slower pieces

questions.explanation: The complaint is about perceived latency, specifically the wait before any output appears. Streaming surfaces tokens as they are generated, so time-to-first-token drops and the reply feels responsive even while the long generation continues. C is the strong runner-up because batching is a real cost/throughput lever, but it loses decisively here: batching is asynchronous with no latency guarantee and would make an interactive chat feel worse, not better. B confuses caching (a cost/prefix-processing optimization) with the delivery mechanism — caching can shave some prefill time but does not stream the output the user is waiting on. D is backwards: raising max output tokens does not make output appear sooner and can lengthen the very wait users are complaining about.

questions.q: A subagent's job is to read three specific log files and report whether a given error signature appears. To make it robust against unforeseen needs, an engineer proposes granting it the full filesystem read tool, a shell-execution tool, and an outbound HTTP tool. From an architecture standpoint, which is the FIRST concern to raise?

options.A: Outbound HTTP access could make the subagent's responses slower and less deterministic, which complicates reproducing results during evaluation runs
options.B: The shell and HTTP tools will materially increase token usage because their schemas are large and consume space in every prompt sent to the subagent
options.C: The broad grants violate least privilege; the subagent should receive only a read capability scoped to the three log paths it actually needs
options.D: Granting many tools raises the chance the model selects an irrelevant tool, lowering task accuracy on the narrow log-inspection objective it was given

questions.explanation: Least privilege is the governing principle: a subagent should hold only the tools its task requires, and a log reader needs a path-scoped read capability, nothing more. C names this directly as the first concern. D is true and a real secondary effect (tool-selection noise), but it is a performance argument, not the primary safety/architecture issue. B and A are also genuine but minor considerations (prompt size, determinism) that miss the central point that shell and unrestricted network access on a read-only task is an unnecessary blast-radius expansion.

questions.q: You are designing how an agent reads a customer's current account balance and account history versus how it moves money. Under MCP conventions, which mapping is correct?

options.A: Expose all three as tools, because anything the model invokes by name should be modeled as a tool for consistency
options.B: Expose all three as resources, since they all concern the same account and share authentication
options.C: Expose balance and history as tools so the model can refresh them on demand, and money movement as a resource
options.D: Expose balance and history as resources (read-only context) and money movement as a tool (side-effecting action)

questions.explanation: MCP draws the line by effect: resources supply read-only context that the host pulls in, tools perform side-effecting actions the model invokes. Balance and history are reads; moving money is a side effect (D). C inverts the semantics and makes a destructive action look like passive context. B mislabels a side-effecting transfer as read-only, hiding its risk. A collapses a meaningful safety distinction; not everything addressable should be a tool.

questions.q: An agent decides whether to approve, escalate, or deny refund requests using a multi-factor policy (amount thresholds, account age, prior refunds, fraud signals). It returns a clean JSON verdict, but on edge cases combining several factors the verdict is frequently wrong, even though each individual rule is stated correctly. Which change is MOST likely to improve correctness on the multi-factor cases?

options.A: Add more few-shot examples of correctly decided simple single-factor cases in order to reinforce the policy the model is meant to apply
options.B: Have the model reason step-by-step through each individual policy factor in turn before it emits the final JSON verdict for the request
options.C: Convert the verdict into a forced tool call with a strict enum so that the output structure of every response is guaranteed to be valid
options.D: Lower the temperature parameter to 0 so that the model applies the refund policy in exactly the same way on every request that arrives

questions.explanation: B is correct: the verdicts are structurally fine but wrong on cases requiring several factors to be weighed together — exactly where chain-of-thought helps, by forcing the model to evaluate each factor before committing. C fixes structure, which is not the problem (output is already clean JSON), so it cannot improve correctness. D removes run-to-run variance, but the errors are reasoning errors on hard combinations, not randomness. A reinforces simple cases the model already handles and does not teach how to combine multiple factors on edge cases.

questions.q: A customer-facing chat assistant sends a 9,000-token policy manual on every turn, followed by the rolling conversation and the user's newest question. Traffic is steady during business hours but goes idle for 20-30 minutes at a time between sessions. The team enables prompt caching to cut cost on the manual. They report the cache rarely seems to help. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.A: The cache expires during the 20-30 minute idle gaps because its TTL is only a few minutes, so each session pays full price again
options.B: The manual sits after the conversation history, so variable turns shift the cached prefix and block reuse across requests
options.C: Prompt caching requires temperature 0, and the assistant runs hotter than that to produce more natural-sounding replies
options.D: Caching stores only the model's final responses, and since each user question differs the stored answers never match the new turn

questions.explanation: The decisive constraint is the traffic pattern: cache TTL is only a few minutes and expires if unused, so 20-30 minute idle gaps guarantee a cold cache at the start of each session, eliminating most of the benefit. B is the strongest runner-up and a real anti-pattern (stable content must come first), but the prompt states the manual is sent first, so ordering is not the fault here. C is false: caching has no temperature-0 requirement. D is false: caching reuses processed prompt prefixes, not final responses.

questions.q: You are about to undertake a large refactor that spans roughly 15 files across three modules and changes a shared interface. You want Claude to lay out the full approach for your review before it edits anything. What is the MOST appropriate FIRST step?

options.A: Configure a PreToolUse hook that denies every Edit call until you manually approve each one of them
options.B: Enter plan mode so Claude researches and proposes a multi-file plan for approval before editing
options.C: Add the refactor steps to CLAUDE.md so the plan persists and Claude follows it across all sessions
options.D: Write a project command in .claude/commands/ that encodes the refactor and then run it immediately

questions.explanation: Plan mode is purpose-built for large, multi-file architectural work: Claude investigates and presents a plan you approve before any edits occur, satisfying the review-before-edits constraint directly. CLAUDE.md (C) is persistent context, not a review gate, and storing a one-off refactor plan there is a misuse. B blanket PreToolUse deny hook (A) could block edits but turns every edit into a friction point and does not produce an upfront reviewable plan; it is a real mechanism applied to the wrong job. B command (D) executes immediately, skipping the review step you explicitly want. A is the tempting runner-up because it 'gates edits,' but it gates execution, not the plan.

questions.q: A nightly job re-classifies 800,000 archived support tickets against a fixed taxonomy. There is no user waiting; results must land in the warehouse before the 8 a.m. report. Cost is the dominant concern and the window is roughly 7 hours. Which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Submit the tickets through the Message Batches API and reconcile each result by its custom_id when the batch completes
options.B: Process tickets one at a time on a single low-latency endpoint to keep memory flat and simplify error handling
options.C: Fan out synchronous streaming requests across many worker threads to finish as fast as possible and retry failures inline
options.D: Embed every ticket into one giant prompt so the shared taxonomy is cached once and reused for the whole corpus

questions.explanation: The workload is non-blocking and overnight, which is exactly what the Batches API targets: about 50% cheaper, no low-latency SLA (results within up to ~24h, well inside 7 hours), with each result correlated by custom_id. C optimizes for speed nobody needs and forfeits the batch discount, making it the suboptimal runner-up. B is far too slow for 800k items and still pays full price. D is unworkable: one prompt cannot hold 800k tickets, and stuffing the context pollutes each classification rather than isolating it.

questions.q: A CI pipeline must run Claude Code non-interactively to triage failing tests and emit a summary to stdout, with no TTY and no human approvals. Which invocation is correct?

options.A: Run claude with the --batch flag so it processes the given prompt without ever prompting the user
options.B: Run claude in interactive mode and pipe `yes` into it to auto-confirm all of the permission prompts
options.C: Run claude -p (--print) with the prompt so it executes non-interactively and prints output
options.D: Set the CLAUDE_HEADLESS=1 environment variable in the CI job and then run claude normally

questions.explanation: Non-interactive headless execution is done with `claude -p`/`--print`, which runs the prompt and writes results to stdout without a TTY. CLAUDE_HEADLESS (D) is not a real environment variable and a --batch flag (A) does not exist; both are plausible-sounding distractors. Piping `yes` (B) is a hack that does not reliably handle the structured permission flow and is not the supported headless path. The trap is that D and A sound like exactly what a CI engineer would reach for, but only C is the actual mechanism.

questions.q: A live dashboard must reclassify 5,000 items every 5 minutes and render the updated labels before the next refresh. An engineer proposes routing the job through the Message Batches API to capture its cost discount. The hard requirement is that results are ready within the 5-minute window, every cycle. What is the correct architecture?

options.A: Use the Message Batches API on the same 5-minute cadence as the dashboard refresh, since batching is by far the cheapest option available and is explicitly designed for exactly this kind of repeating, fixed-size classification workload
options.B: Raise the model's context window so all 5,000 items can be classified in a single request per cycle, eliminating the need for either batching or parallel calls
options.C: Keep using the Message Batches API but submit a fresh batch every 30 seconds, so that overlapping in-flight batches keep results flowing fast enough to meet the deadline
options.D: Issue the 5,000 classifications as parallel synchronous requests within each cycle, accepting the higher per-call cost because the batch turnaround cannot guarantee delivery inside the 5-minute SLA

questions.explanation: The decisive constraint is the 5-minute SLA per cycle. The Batches API offers no latency guarantee and can take up to ~24h, so it cannot reliably meet a tight, repeating deadline — the discount is irrelevant if the data is late. Parallel synchronous calls give predictable, near-real-time turnaround and are the right tradeoff despite higher cost. A is the strong runner-up (batching genuinely fits a repeating fixed-size workload and is cheapest) but loses precisely on the SLA: cheapest is not usable if it misses the window. B is false — the context window cannot be raised on the fly, and cramming 5,000 items into one call does not fix latency. C misunderstands batch SLAs: stacking more batches does not bound any single batch's completion time, so deadlines can still be missed.

questions.q: A pipeline assigns ICD-10 medical billing codes from clinical notes. Accuracy is mediocre: the model picks plausible-sounding but wrong codes and uses loose lay vocabulary instead of precise clinical terms. The team keeps appending more individual coding facts and edge cases into the user turn, but accuracy barely moves. They want the model to reason with the right domain framing and vocabulary. What is the MOST effective change?

options.A: Set an explicit system role establishing the model as an experienced certified medical coder, grounding its vocabulary and domain priors for the task
options.B: Ask the model to list three candidate codes with confidence scores and then pick the highest-scoring one as its final answer for each note
options.C: Lower the temperature to 0 so the model consistently selects the same code for a given note and stops varying between plausible alternatives
options.D: Continue expanding the user turn with as many additional specific ICD-10 facts, documentation exceptions, and individual code mappings as can possibly fit into the available context window for the request

questions.explanation: A wins because a system role activates the domain priors and precise vocabulary of a medical coder across the whole task, which is exactly the framing the output is missing. D is the strong runner-up — relevant domain facts do help — but the team has already shown that piling more facts into the user turn yields little, because the problem is framing and vocabulary, not a missing fact list. C only stabilizes which wrong code gets chosen; consistency is not accuracy. B adds a self-scoring ritual whose confidence numbers are unreliable and does nothing to correct the underlying domain grounding.

questions.q: You maintain a slash command that scaffolds a new service. You want it available to every teammate who clones the repo, version-controlled alongside the code. Where should it live?

options.A: In a .claude/rules/ file with glob frontmatter so it activates whenever service files are being edited
options.B: In your personal ~/.claude/commands/ directory so it is always available wherever you run Claude Code
options.C: In the project's settings.local.json under a commands key so it stays personal and stays uncommitted
options.D: In the project's .claude/commands/ directory, committed to the repository so every clone receives it

questions.explanation: Shared, repo-versioned commands belong in the project's .claude/commands/ directory, which is committed and available to everyone who clones. ~/.claude/commands/ (B) is the personal location, available only to you across projects, so it fails the shared requirement. Rules (A) are for path-based conventions, not invokable scaffolding commands. settings.local.json (C) is explicitly personal and uncommitted, the opposite of shareable, and it is not where commands are defined. B is the strong runner-up because it 'always works for you,' but the decisive lever is shared-with-the-team, which only D delivers.

questions.q: An architect is structuring prompts for a high-traffic API where each request shares a large stable system prompt and tool schema but ends with a short, request-specific user query. All of the following help maximize prompt cache hit rates EXCEPT:

options.A: Keeping request volume steady enough that the cached prefix is reused before its short TTL expires
options.B: Inserting a unique request identifier near the top of each prompt for easier log correlation
options.C: Keeping the per-request variable content, such as the user's query, at the end of the prompt
options.D: Placing the stable system prompt and tool schema at the very start of every request

questions.explanation: Caching reuses a matching processed prefix, so putting a unique identifier near the top changes the prefix on every request and defeats cache reuse; that is the EXCEPT answer. D and C follow the core rule of stable-content-first, variable-last to maximize the shared prefix. A is also genuinely helpful because the short TTL means steady reuse keeps the entry warm. Only B actively undermines caching.

questions.q: During a session Claude proposes running `terraform apply`. You deny the permission because you want a `plan` first. Which statement correctly describes what should happen next?

options.A: The denial is just a temporary block, so Claude should immediately retry the same apply command this turn
options.B: Approving any later command in the session permanently whitelists terraform apply for all future sessions
options.C: The denial means refusal of that action, so Claude should adjust course (e.g., run terraform plan) instead
options.D: The denial pauses the harness until you edit settings.local.json to allow terraform apply before continuing

questions.explanation: A denied permission call is a refusal of that specific action; the correct response is to adjust the plan, not to re-issue the identical command. A (retry) and B (one approval is permanent across sessions) are both wrong because approving a permission once is not permanent and does not whitelist future runs. D invents a hard requirement to hand-edit settings.local.json mid-flow; while settings.local.json does hold personal allow-rules, the harness does not block waiting on it after a single denial. C is correct; A and B are the confident distractors that misstate permission persistence.

questions.q: You must search a large, noisy codebase (thousands of log files and test outputs) for a single missing configuration value, and you are worried the raw search results will flood your main session's context with junk. How should you structure this so the noise stays out of the main context?

options.A: Run the search normally and then use /compact afterward to summarize the session so the accumulated noise is compressed away.
options.B: Dispatch the search to a subagent that runs in its own separate context window and returns only the distilled result to the main session.
options.C: Enter plan mode first so Claude reads the files carefully and avoids pulling the noisy search results into the working session at all.
options.D: Run Claude Code in headless mode with --output-format json so the search output comes back compact and machine-readable instead of verbose.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. C subagent runs in its own isolated context window, so the thousands of noisy log lines and false positives stay in the subagent's context and only a short summary returns to the main session. Runner-up is C: plan mode is genuinely useful, but it only prevents edits—it does not isolate context, and all the noisy files would still be read into the main session. A compresses history after the damage is done rather than preventing the bloat. D is about programmatic invocation and does nothing for in-session context isolation.

questions.q: A production pipeline extracts invoice data and feeds a downstream system that rejects any payload not valid against a fixed JSON Schema. The current prompt ends with "Return only the JSON object, no other text." Roughly 4% of responses still carry a markdown fence or a trailing sentence, breaking the consumer. The team needs the integration to stop failing on malformed payloads. Which change is MOST reliable?

options.A: Restate the format rule more forcefully as "Output MUST be raw JSON only; any prose or code fence is a critical error" and pin it at both the top and bottom of the prompt
options.B: Add three few-shot examples that each demonstrate the exact bare-JSON output and nothing else, placed immediately before the user input each request
options.C: Set the temperature parameter to 0 so the model deterministically follows the existing output-format instruction on every single request it receives
options.D: Define the extraction as a tool whose input is that JSON Schema, force the tool, and validate the emitted tool call at the tool layer, retrying on any failure

questions.explanation: D is correct: forced tool use with a schema validated at the tool layer plus retry makes the type contract enforceable rather than hoped-for, which is exactly what a hard-rejecting consumer needs. A is the obvious fix already in place (a prose instruction) just made louder — it cannot guarantee structure and the 4% rate shows instruction-strengthening has plateaued. B (few-shot) raises adherence but still produces free text that can drift and is unvalidated, so malformed payloads can still reach the consumer. C reduces randomness but does not enforce a schema or instruction-following; deterministic does not mean conformant.

questions.q: An agent provisions cloud infrastructure and one of its steps permanently deletes a customer's production database to recreate it from a new template. The team wants a human approval gate. Currently the agent plans the whole run, gets one human sign-off on the overall plan at the start, then executes everything autonomously including the delete. Where should the approval gate go to best protect against an irreversible mistake?

options.A: Place a blocking human approval immediately before the delete-database step, pausing execution at that point and proceeding only on explicit confirmation
options.B: Add an automated safety-check subagent that re-validates the deletion's preconditions right before it runs and approves the step when they all pass
options.C: Keep the single up-front sign-off on the full plan, since approving the complete sequence in advance covers every step including the deletion
options.D: Run the deletion automatically but require human review of an audit summary immediately after it completes, so a person can intervene the moment anything looks wrong

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is irreversibility: the gate must sit immediately before the destructive step so a human approves the action with the most current state, when stopping is still possible. The strong runner-up is C (up-front plan approval): reviewing the plan early is reasonable governance, but state can drift between approval and execution, and a human rubber-stamping a long plan rarely registers the one catastrophic line — the gate is too far from the action. D loses outright because reviewing after the delete is hopeless: the database is already gone and there is nothing to intervene on. B loses because an automated check can confirm preconditions but cannot supply the human judgment the team explicitly required for an irreversible action.

questions.q: A research agent is told to 'gather competitive pricing until you have enough confidence.' It repeatedly calls a paid web-scraping tool, each time deciding it needs 'just a bit more data,' and on one run made 600 paid calls before a human noticed the bill. The team needs a hard guarantee that no run can exceed a fixed budget. What is the most reliable fix?

options.A: Switch the scraping tool to a cheaper provider so that even a long run stays within an acceptable total cost even if the agent keeps looping
options.B: Rewrite the prompt to tell the agent to stop after it has gathered sufficient data and to be mindful of cost, with an explicit reminder to avoid redundant calls
options.C: Add a deterministic iteration cap / circuit breaker in code that hard-stops the loop after N tool calls regardless of what the model decides
options.D: Add an evaluator subagent that periodically judges whether enough data has been collected and instructs the main agent to stop when it agrees

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is the need for a hard guarantee: only a deterministic counter enforced in code can make 'no run exceeds the budget' true regardless of model behavior. The strong runner-up is D (an evaluator that signals stop): an evaluator-optimizer can improve stopping decisions, but it is itself a model whose judgment can be wrong or be ignored, so it provides no guaranteed ceiling — and it adds cost. B loses because prompt-only guidance is exactly what already failed; the model kept rationalizing 'a bit more.' A loses because cheaper-per-call does not bound the number of calls, so an unbounded loop can still blow any budget — it treats the unit price, not the runaway count.

questions.q: An assistant's prompt is assembled in this order: a tiny per-call config block (current timestamp plus the requesting user's locale, ~40 tokens that change every call), then a 7,000-token stable system prompt and tool catalog that never changes, then the user's question. The team enabled prompt caching but sees almost no cache hits across calls. Which fix recovers cache reuse?

options.A: Lower the temperature to 0, since prompt caching only reuses a prefix when the model is sampling deterministically
options.B: Move the stable 7,000-token system prompt and tool catalog to the front so it forms the cached prefix, and push the volatile config block to just before the user's question
options.C: Leave the current order untouched but raise the cache TTL substantially, on the theory that the small leading config block will then stay warm long enough to be reused across consecutive calls
options.D: Drop the per-call timestamp and locale entirely, because any dynamic value anywhere in the prompt makes caching impossible for the whole request

questions.explanation: Caching reuses the longest unchanged PREFIX. With volatile content first, the prefix changes every call and the large stable block can never be cached. Reordering so the 7,000-token stable block leads (and the config goes last, before the question) makes that big segment a reusable prefix. D is the strong runner-up: removing the dynamic values would also restore reuse, but it loses because the timestamp and locale are legitimately needed for behavior — you should not delete required context when simple reordering keeps both the data and the cache benefit. A is a known false statement: caching does not require temperature 0. C misunderstands the mechanic — a longer TTL cannot help when the cached segment is a 40-token block that changes every single call; the problem is ordering, not expiry.

questions.q: You need to guarantee that a `git push --force` to the main branch can never execute, and you separately want to validate and reformat the output of safe git commands after they run. Which hook timing handles each job?

options.A: Use PreToolUse to deny git push --force before it runs, and PostToolUse to validate and reformat the safe git command output afterward.
options.B: Use a PreToolUse hook for both jobs, since a single pre-execution hook can deny dangerous commands and also validate the safe ones before they run.
options.C: Use PostToolUse to block the force push by catching it right after it fails, and PreToolUse to validate the safe commands before they execute.
options.D: Use a PostToolUse hook for both jobs, since a single post-execution hook can deny the dangerous push and also inspect the output of the safe commands.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A. PreToolUse fires before execution and is the only hook that can deny a tool call outright, so blocking a force push must happen there; PostToolUse fires after a command succeeds, which is exactly when you can inspect and reformat its output. Runner-up is B, which is partly right—PreToolUse can indeed deny the dangerous command—but it cannot validate output that does not exist yet, so the after-the-fact reformatting still needs PostToolUse. D is wrong because PostToolUse cannot prevent execution. C is backwards: a hook that fires after execution cannot be your blocking mechanism.

questions.q: A lead agent dispatches subagents to draft sections of a compliance report. One subagent must apply a redaction policy to personally identifiable information before its section is merged. Auditors require that redaction provably occurs on every run, with zero tolerance for leakage. The current design relies on the subagent's prompt to redact. What should the architect change FIRST?

options.A: Strengthen the subagent's prompt with explicit redaction rules and examples, and add a reminder of the policy in the lead agent's merge instructions
options.B: Lower the subagent's temperature and pin the model version so redaction behavior stays consistent and reproducible across every report it generates
options.C: Apply redaction in a deterministic post-processing function over each section's text, so unredacted PII cannot reach the merged report regardless of model behavior
options.D: Add a second reviewer subagent that re-reads each section and flags any PII the first subagent missed before the lead merges the content together

questions.explanation: Zero-tolerance, auditable redaction is a critical guarantee, and guarantees come from deterministic enforcement, not prompt compliance. C places redaction in code that runs unconditionally, so leakage is structurally prevented and provable to auditors. D is the strongest runner-up and adds defense in depth, but it is still a probabilistic model checking another model and cannot certify zero leakage. A and B only tune the likelihood of correct model behavior; neither can satisfy a zero-tolerance, provable requirement. The lever is compliance plus irreversibility of a leak.

questions.q: A research agent fans out to four subagents, each reading a large document set. The lead agent's context window keeps overflowing because each subagent returns its full retrieved passages plus reasoning traces. Throughput is fine; the only problem is the lead exhausting context. Which change addresses the root cause?

options.A: Persist each subagent's full output to a shared store and pass the lead only file references it can selectively re-read when required
options.B: Run the four subagents sequentially instead of in parallel so their outputs arrive one at a time and can be summarized between calls
options.C: Increase the lead agent's context window to the largest available size so all four subagent outputs fit comfortably with headroom to spare
options.D: Have each subagent return only a distilled conclusion with the specific citations the lead needs, omitting raw passages and intermediate reasoning

questions.explanation: The root cause is that subagents dump raw context upward; the correct contract is that a subagent returns a distilled conclusion, not its working set. D fixes this directly. C merely defers the overflow and raises cost/latency without addressing the dump. B changes scheduling, not payload size, and needlessly sacrifices parallelism for independent tasks. A is the plausible runner-up and can help, but it adds an external store and re-read round-trips when the real fix is simply having subagents summarize before returning; A treats the symptom (where bytes live) rather than the contract violation.

questions.q: A nightly job converts uploaded invoices to a fixed format: validate schema → extract totals → apply the standard tax rule → write a normalized record. The steps are identical every run, fully specified, with no branching or judgment required. The team currently runs this as a ReAct agent that reasons about what to do at each step, and they see occasional skipped steps and higher latency and token cost than expected. What should they switch to?

options.A: Keep ReAct but add a reflection step after each action where the agent verifies it followed the prescribed pipeline before continuing
options.B: An orchestrator that spawns a subagent per step and coordinates them, so each step is owned by a dedicated specialized agent for reliability
options.C: A predefined deterministic workflow that always executes the four fixed steps in order in code, calling the model only where genuine extraction is needed
options.D: Keep the ReAct loop but constrain it with a detailed system prompt that enumerates the four steps and their exact required order, plus several few-shot examples of correct end-to-end runs so the model reliably follows the prescribed sequence

questions.explanation: The decisive lever is that the pipeline is fully known and fixed with no branching, so a deterministic workflow is more reliable, cheaper, and lower-latency than open-ended agent reasoning — you only invoke the model for the genuinely fuzzy extraction. The strong runner-up is D (constraining the ReAct loop): tightening the prompt can reduce errors, but it keeps per-step LLM reasoning that is unnecessary for fixed steps, so it still pays the latency and token cost and still permits skipped steps probabilistically. B loses because multi-agent orchestration adds coordination overhead and cost to a simple linear task — over-engineering. A loses because adding reflection multiplies model calls and cost for steps that never needed reasoning in the first place.

questions.q: A classifier sorts support tickets into "billing," "technical," and "account-access." It handles clear cases well but is inconsistent on tickets about a password reset needed in order to view an invoice — sometimes billing, sometimes account-access. The label wording is unambiguous and the model clearly understands each category in isolation. Which fix best addresses the inconsistency?

options.A: Set the temperature parameter to 0 to remove the run-to-run variability that makes the very same ticket sometimes land in two completely different output categories here
options.B: Add explicit decomposed criteria for the boundary region plus a contrasting few-shot pair showing a password-reset-to-view-invoice ticket and its correct label
options.C: Raise the number of few-shot examples for billing and account-access from two each up to eight each so the model is exposed to far more variety
options.D: Instruct the model to first emit its chain-of-thought reasoning and only then the label, so that it deliberates carefully before committing to a decision
